        "tool_requirements": ["samtools"],
        "applicable_tags": ["fasta", "fasta_gz"],
        "source_id": "sarscov2_fasta",
        "output_pattern": "{sample_id}.fasta"
      },
      "derive_bam": {
        "template": "derive_bam.sh.j2",
        "tool_requirements": ["samtools"],
        "applicable_tags": ["bam", "cram", "sam"],
        "source_id": "sarscov2_bam",
        "output_pattern": "{sample_id}_sorted.bam"
      },
      "derive_bed": {
        "template": "derive_bed.sh.j2",
        "tool_requirements": [],
        "applicable_tags": ["bed"],
        "source_id": "sarscov2_bed",
        "output_pattern": "{sample_id}.bed"
      }
    },
    "chain_modules": {
      "bwa_index": {
        "module": "modules/nf-core/bwa/index/main.nf",
        "process_name": "BWA_INDEX",
        "test_input_path": "genomics/sarscov2/genome/genome.fasta",
        "output_channel": "index",
        "produces_tags": ["bwa_index", "genome_index"]
      },
      "samtools_faidx": {
        "module": "modules/nf-core/samtools/faidx/main.nf",
        "process_name": "SAMTOOLS_FAIDX",
        "test_input_path": "genomics/sarscov2/genome/genome.fasta",
        "output_channel": "fai",
        "produces_tags": ["fasta_index", "fai"]
      },
      "samtools_sort": {
        "module": "modules/nf-core/samtools/sort/main.nf",
        "process_name": "SAMTOOLS_SORT",
        "test_input_path": "genomics/sarscov2/illumina/bam/test.bam",
        "output_channel": "bam",
        "produces_tags": ["bam", "sorted_bam"]
      }
    }
  }
}

Also create src/code_to_module/standards/data/STANDARDS_CHANGELOG.md
(ships inside the package alongside the JSON schema, not in the repo root):
# Standards Changelog
## 3.5.0 — 2026-02-01
- Initial schema. Encodes nf-core/tools 3.5.0 conventions.
- Topic channels for versions (replaces versions.yml approach).

--- PART 2: Implement src/code_to_module/standards/ subpackage ---

Create src/code_to_module/standards/loader.py and export from
src/code_to_module/standards/__init__.py:

  # standards/__init__.py
  from code_to_module.standards.loader import Standards, get_standards
  __all__ = ["Standards", "get_standards"]

  # All other modules import as:
  from code_to_module.standards import get_standards

  # standards/loader.py — the actual implementation:
  class Standards:
      """
      Loads and provides typed access to nf_core_standards.json.
      Checks for staleness at import time (unless disabled).
      """

      def __init__(self, schema_path: Path | None = None):
          """Load from bundled schema by default."""
          ...

      # Typed accessors — never let callers read raw JSON
      @property
      def valid_labels(self) -> list[str]: ...

      @property
      def label_resources(self) -> dict[str, dict]: ...

      @property
      def required_output_channels(self) -> list[str]: ...

      @property
      def versions_use_topic_channels(self) -> bool: ...

      @property
      def docker_registry(self) -> str: ...

      @property
      def singularity_registry(self) -> str: ...

      @property
      def conda_channels(self) -> list[str]: ...

      @property
      def meta_yml_required_fields(self) -> list[str]: ...

      @property
      def edam_for(self) -> dict[str, str]:
          """Return EDAM ontology mapping dict.""" ...

      @property
      def known_tools(self) -> list[str]:
          """Return list of tools with known Bioconda packages for Tier 1-2 assessment.""" ...

      @property
      def tier_thresholds(self) -> dict[str, float]:
          """Return confidence thresholds for tier assignment and pre-selection.""" ...

      @property
      def helper_filename_patterns(self) -> list[str]:
          """Return filename substrings that identify non-entrypoint files.""" ...

      @property
      def helper_dirname_patterns(self) -> list[str]:
          """Return directory names that contain non-entrypoint files.""" ...

      @property
      def test_data_base_path(self) -> str: ...

      @property
      def test_data_index(self) -> list[dict]:
          """Return the full test data index array.""" ...

      def find_test_data(self, tags: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
          """Return index entries whose tags are a superset of given tags,
          sorted by size_kb ascending.""" ...

      @property
      def derivation_templates(self) -> dict[str, dict]:
          ...returns derivation_templates dict from schema...

      def derivation_template_for(self, format_tags: list[str]) -> dict | None:
          ...returns first entry whose applicable_tags intersects format_tags, or None...

      @property
      def chain_modules(self) -> dict[str, dict]:
          ...returns chain_modules dict from schema...

      def chain_module_for(self, format_tags: list[str]) -> dict | None:
          ...returns first entry whose produces_tags is superset of format_tags, or None...

      @property
      def schema_version(self) -> str: ...

      @property
      def last_updated(self) -> str: ...

      def is_stale(self, max_age_days: int = 30) -> bool:
          """Return True if last_updated is more than max_age_days ago.""" ...

      def check_for_updates(self, timeout: int = 5) -> str | None:
          """
          Fetch source_url, compare schema_version.
          Return new version string if available, None if up to date or unreachable.
          Never raises — network errors return None silently.
          """
          ...

      @classmethod
      def fetch_and_save(cls, timeout: int = 10) -> "Standards":
          """
          Download latest schema from source_url, validate it has required
          top-level keys, save to bundled path, return loaded instance.
          Raises ValueError if downloaded schema is malformed.
          """
          ...

  # Module-level singleton in loader.py — import via __init__.py
  _standards_instance: Standards | None = None

  def get_standards() -> Standards:
      global _standards_instance
      if _standards_instance is None:
          _standards_instance = Standards()
      return _standards_instance

  # Standards() uses importlib.resources to locate the bundled JSON:
  #   from importlib.resources import files
  #   schema_path = files("code_to_module.standards.data") / "nf_core_standards.json"
  # This works correctly when installed as a package (pip install) and when
  # running from source, unlike __file__-relative paths.

Now update every other module to use get_standards() instead of hardcoded values.
All imports must use: from code_to_module.standards import get_standards
(not from code_to_module.standards.loader — always go through __init__.py):
  - assess.py: use standards.known_tools instead of the hardcoded list literal;
               use standards.tier_thresholds for confidence thresholds;
               use standards.helper_filename_patterns and helper_dirname_patterns
  - discover.py: use standards.helper_filename_patterns and helper_dirname_patterns
                 in _build_manifest entrypoint detection;
                 use standards.tier_thresholds["pre_select_min_confidence"] for
                 pre_selected assignment on FunctionalitySpec
  - infer.py: use standards.valid_labels in system prompt, standards.edam_for in
              meta.yml generation, standards.docker_registry / singularity_registry
  - generate.py: use standards.conda_channels in environment.yml template context,
                 standards.versions_use_topic_channels to switch template blocks
  - test_data_match.py: use standards.test_data_index and standards.find_test_data()
  - test_data_derive.py: use standards.derivation_templates,
                         standards.derivation_template_for(),
                         standards.chain_modules, standards.chain_module_for()
  - test_gen.py: use standards.test_data_base_path for URL construction
  - templates/*.j2: receive standards values via template context dict, not hardcoded

--- PART 3: Implement update-standards CLI command ---

Add to src/code_to_module/cli.py:

  @main.command("update-standards")
  @click.option("--check-only", is_flag=True,
                help="Check if update available without downloading")
  def update_standards_cmd(check_only: bool):
      """Check for and apply updates to the bundled nf-core standards schema."""

Behaviour:
  1. Load current schema, print: "Current: v{schema_version} ({last_updated})"
  2. Call standards.check_for_updates():
     - If None (up to date or unreachable): print "✓ Schema is up to date" and exit 0
     - If network unreachable: print "⚠ Could not reach update server — using bundled schema"
       and exit 0 (never fail loudly on network issues)
     - If new version available: print "⚡ Update available: v{new_version}"
  3. If --check-only: exit 0
  4. Prompt: "Update to v{new_version}? [y/N]:"
     If yes: call Standards.fetch_and_save()
       On success: print "✓ Standards updated to v{new_version}"
       On ValueError (malformed schema): print error, keep old schema, exit 1
     If no: exit 0

--- Staleness check at startup ---
In cli.py convert command, BEFORE step 1 (welcome panel), unless --no-update-check:
  s = get_standards()
  if s.is_stale(max_age_days=30):
      new_version = s.check_for_updates(timeout=3)
      if new_version:
          console.print(f"[yellow]⚠ nf-core standards update available: v{new_version}[/yellow]")
          console.print("[dim]  Run: code-to-module update-standards[/dim]")
  # Never block on this — always continue

Run the tests written in Prompt 5:
  pytest tests/test_standards.py -v

All tests must pass before this prompt is considered complete.
In particular, test_no_hardcoded_labels must pass — if it fails, find and remove
the hardcoded values before proceeding to Prompt 7.
All HTTP calls mocked. Never write to real bundled schema path — use tmp_path fixture.

docs.title: Prompt 7 — Complexity Assessment (Tiers 1–5)
docs.text:
Goal: Before any LLM inference call, assess how confidently we can generate a module for a specific functionality. Runs once per selected FunctionalitySpec.

Implement src/code_to_module/assess.py.

Export: def assess(func: FunctionalitySpec, source: CodeSource) -> tuple[int, float, list[str]]
Returns: (tier, confidence_0_to_1, list_of_warning_messages)

The assessment operates on func.code_section (the extracted code for this specific
functionality), not source.raw_code. This matters for multi-functionality repos where
one subcommand may be Tier 1 and another Tier 4.

Tier assignment logic (check in order, assign lowest matching tier):

Tier 1 — High confidence, known tool:
  - func.code_section language is bash or python
  - code_section contains exactly one external tool call from known_tools list
  - that tool has a Bioconda package (GET https://api.anaconda.org/package/bioconda/{tool})
  - func.detection_method is CLICK_DECORATOR, ARGPARSE_SUBPARSER, or SHELL_CASE_STATEMENT
  → confidence: 0.90+, inherit func.confidence if lower

Tier 2 — Good confidence, known tool with complexity:
  - Same as Tier 1 but: multiple output patterns detected, OR optional flags present,
    OR tool in bio.tools API (https://bio.tools/api/tool/{tool}/)
  - OR detection_method is MULTI_SCRIPT_REPO with a known tool
  → confidence: 0.75–0.89

Tier 3 — Moderate confidence, custom script:
  - Python/R/Perl with inferrable I/O (argparse/click/optparse arguments present in
    code_section, OR input/output variable names clearly identifiable)
  - No Bioconda entry found, but language and purpose are clear
  - func.detection_method is any (rule-based or LLM)
  → confidence: 0.50–0.74, add warning: "No container found — stub will be generated"

Tier 4 — Low confidence, complex custom code:
  - code_section calls multiple external tools
  - OR no clear argument parsing in code_section
  - OR code_section > 500 lines
  - OR code_section imports non-standard libraries with no Bioconda equivalent
  - OR func.detection_method == LLM_INFERENCE AND func.confidence < 0.65
  → confidence: 0.25–0.49, add warnings for each ambiguity

Tier 5 — Cannot proceed:
  - language is "unknown" OR code_section is empty / < 5 lines
  - OR func.confidence == 0.0
  → confidence: 0.0, warning explaining why

known_tools: always read from standards.known_tools.
The assessment key already exists in nf_core_standards.json (added in Prompt 6).
Do NOT use a hardcoded fallback list — if standards fails to load, raise an error
rather than silently using stale values. The no-hardcoding rule from Prompt 6 applies here.

Use httpx for API calls. Cache per tool name (not per call site).
Print a Rich panel for each functionality assessed:
  "Assessing: {func.display_name} [{func.detection_method.value}]"
  Show tier badge (green=1-2, yellow=3, orange=4, red=5), confidence %, warnings.

Tests in tests/test_assess.py:
  - Use FunctionalitySpec fixtures directly (not CodeSource)
  - test_tier1_known_tool: code_section calls samtools, Bioconda mock returns 200 → Tier 1
  - test_tier2_known_tool_complex: multiple outputs → Tier 2
  - test_tier3_custom_python: argparse present, no Bioconda match → Tier 3
  - test_tier4_no_structure: long script, no argument parsing → Tier 4
  - test_tier5_empty: empty code_section → Tier 5
  - test_uses_standards_known_tools: assert known_tools read from standards, not hardcoded
  - test_multi_functionality_mixed: two FunctionalitySpecs from same source →
    one Tier 1, one Tier 4 (different tiers for same repo)
  Mock all HTTP calls.

docs.title: Prompt 8 — LLM Inference Engine
docs.text:
Goal: Use the Anthropic API to infer nf-core channel structure for one specific functionality. Scoped to func.code_section, not the full repo.

Implement src/code_to_module/infer.py.

Export:
  async def infer_module_spec(
      func: FunctionalitySpec,
      source: CodeSource,
      tier: int,
  ) -> ModuleSpec

  def infer_module_spec_sync(func, source, tier) -> ModuleSpec

This function calls the Anthropic API once per FunctionalitySpec.
It uses func.code_section (the extracted code block for this functionality)
rather than source.raw_code, so the LLM sees only what's relevant.

For multi-functionality repos, this function is called N times (once per
selected functionality), potentially in parallel using asyncio.gather().

System prompt (store as constant SYSTEM_PROMPT):
"""
You are an nf-core module expert. Given a bioinformatics code section, extract
the information needed to create an nf-core DSL2 module following nf-core/tools
3.5+ conventions.

You MUST respond with valid JSON only — no markdown, no explanation, just JSON.

Required JSON structure:
{
  "tool_name": "lowercase_tool_name",
  "process_name": "TOOLNAME_SUBCOMMAND",
  "inputs": [
    {
      "name": "meta",
      "type": "map",
      "description": "Sample metadata map",
      "pattern": null,
      "optional": false,
      "format_tags": [],
      "test_data_strategy": "standard_format"
    },
    {
      "name": "reads",
      "type": "file",
      "description": "Input FASTQ reads",
      "pattern": "*.{fastq,fastq.gz,fq,fq.gz}",
      "optional": false,
      "format_tags": ["fastq", "paired_end"],
      "test_data_strategy": "standard_format"
    }
  ],
  "outputs": [
    {
      "name": "bam",
      "type": "file",
      "description": "Aligned BAM file",
      "pattern": "*.bam",
      "optional": false,
      "format_tags": ["bam"],
      "test_data_strategy": "standard_format"
    },
    {
      "name": "versions",
      "type": "file",
      "description": "Software versions",
      "pattern": "versions.yml",
      "optional": false,
      "format_tags": [],
      "test_data_strategy": "standard_format"
    }
  ],
  "label": "process_medium",
  "ext_args": "",
  "container_search_term": "tool_name",
  "conda_package": "bioconda::tool_name=1.0.0",
  "warnings": []
}

Rules:
- Always include meta as first input (type: map)
- Always include versions as last output
- process_name uses the TOOL_SUBCOMMAND convention passed in the user prompt
- label: process_single (≤1 CPU, ≤6GB), process_medium (≤4 CPU, ≤12GB),
         process_high (≤12 CPU, ≤24GB), process_high_memory (≤16 CPU, ≤60GB)
- If you cannot determine something, add it to warnings list
- file patterns use glob syntax: *.{bam,cram} not *.bam|*.cram
- Use inferred_inputs and inferred_outputs from the user prompt as strong hints;
  refine them into proper ChannelSpec objects with patterns and format_tags
- Output files MUST use the nf-core prefix convention: name output files using
  a "prefix" variable set from task.ext.prefix ?: meta.id. In Nextflow script:
    def prefix = task.ext.prefix ?: "${meta.id}"
  Output files then reference: "${prefix}.bam", "${prefix}.vcf.gz" etc.
  Add a warning to warnings[] if the source tool uses fixed output filenames that
  will need manual adjustment to follow this convention.

For format_tags, use any combination of:
  fastq, paired_end, single_end, bam, cram, sam, bai, crai, fasta, fasta_index,
  vcf, vcf_gz, tbi, bed, gtf, gff, tsv, csv, txt, json, rds, h5, h5ad,
  count_matrix, genome_index, annotation

For test_data_strategy, classify each input channel as ONE of:
  "standard_format" — well-known bioinformatics format likely in nf-core/test-datasets
  "generatable"     — known format that may need synthetic generation
  "custom"          — proprietary, tool-specific, or undocumented format
  "unknown"         — cannot determine from the code

Never classify meta or versions channels — leave their test_data_strategy as "standard_format".
"""

User prompt template:
"""
Tool name: {func.name}
Functionality: {func.display_name}
Description: {func.description}
Script filename: {source.filename}
Language: {source.language}
Tier: {tier} (1=simple known tool, 3=custom script, 5=unknown)
Detection method: {func.detection_method.value}
Known inputs (from CLI parsing): {', '.join(func.inferred_inputs) or 'unknown'}
Known outputs (from CLI parsing): {', '.join(func.inferred_outputs) or 'unknown'}

{existing_modules_section}  ← omit entirely if source.existing_modules is empty

Relevant code:
{func.code_section}

Extract the nf-core module specification for this specific functionality as JSON.
"""

Construct existing_modules_section as follows:
  If source.existing_modules is non-empty and any have load_error=None:
    section = "Existing modules for this tool family (use for consistency):\n"
    For each ExistingModule with load_error=None:
      section += f"- {mod.process_name}:\n"
      section += f"  container: {mod.container_docker}\n"
      section += f"  label: {mod.label}\n"
      section += f"  inputs: {mod.inputs}\n"
      section += f"  outputs: {mod.outputs}\n"
    section += ("Use the same container URL and version tag, the same label, and "
                "consistent channel naming conventions as the existing modules above. "
                "Only deviate if the new functionality genuinely requires different "
                "resources or a different container.")
  If all ExistingModules have load_error set: omit section entirely.

Populate ModuleSpec.functionality_name = func.name.

The inferred_inputs and inferred_outputs from FunctionalitySpec are HINTS only —
the LLM may refine them into proper ChannelSpec objects with patterns and format_tags.

After getting the JSON response:
1. Parse with json.loads() in try/except.
   On parse failure: log warning, return a Tier-5-style ModuleSpec with error in warnings.
2. Container lookup is NOT done here — container.py handles that.
   Set container_docker and container_singularity to "" (empty strings) for now.
   cli.py calls container.resolve() after this function and updates the ModuleSpec.
3. Validate that process_name follows TOOL_SUBCOMMAND convention:
   If func.name is a subcommand (detection_method != SINGLE_SCRIPT):
     process_name should be {tool}_{func.name.upper()}, e.g. MYTOOL_ALIGN
   If SINGLE_SCRIPT: process_name = TOOLNAME (no subcommand suffix)
4. Return populated ModuleSpec.

For parallel inference (multiple functionalities):
  async def infer_all(
      funcs: list[FunctionalitySpec],
      source: CodeSource,
      tiers: dict[str, int],   ← {func.name: tier}
  ) -> list[ModuleSpec]:
      results = await asyncio.gather(*[
          infer_module_spec(f, source, tiers[f.name]) for f in funcs
      ], return_exceptions=True)
      # Per-call error handling: a timeout or API error on one functionality
      # must not cancel the other calls. Replace exceptions with Tier-5 stubs.
      specs = []
      for func, result in zip(funcs, results):
          if isinstance(result, Exception):
              # Log warning, continue with stub
              stub = ModuleSpec.tier5_stub(func.name)
              stub.warnings.append(f"LLM inference failed: {type(result).__name__}: {result}")
              specs.append(stub)
          else:
              specs.append(result)
      return specs

  def infer_all_sync(funcs, source, tiers) -> list[ModuleSpec]

Use anthropic.AsyncAnthropic client. Model: claude-sonnet-4-20250514, max_tokens: 1000.

Tests in tests/test_infer.py:
  - test_single_functionality: FunctionalitySpec with code_section → ModuleSpec returned
  - test_process_name_subcommand: detection_method=CLICK_DECORATOR, name="align" →
    process_name starts with uppercase tool name + "_ALIGN"
  - test_process_name_single_script: SINGLE_SCRIPT → no underscore suffix in process_name
  - test_inferred_inputs_used_as_hints: func.inferred_inputs passed in user prompt,
    assert they appear in prompt text sent to API
  - test_parallel_inference: infer_all_sync with 3 funcs → 3 ModuleSpecs returned,
    API called 3 times
  - test_parse_failure_returns_tier5_spec: mock API returns invalid JSON →
    ModuleSpec with tier=5 and warning message
  - test_existing_modules_in_prompt: source.existing_modules has one ExistingModule
    with load_error=None → assert prompt text sent to API contains container URL
    and process_name from that ExistingModule
  - test_existing_modules_load_error_omitted: ExistingModule with load_error set →
    section omitted from prompt, no crash
  - test_existing_modules_empty_omitted: source.existing_modules=[] →
    prompt does not contain "Existing modules" section
  Mock Anthropic client. Never make real API calls in tests.

docs.title: Prompt 9 — Jinja2 Templates
docs.text:
Goal: Create the four template files that render the actual module output.

Create the five Jinja2 templates in src/code_to_module/templates/.

--- TEMPLATE 1: main.nf.j2 ---
Generate a valid nf-core DSL2 process following nf-core/tools 3.5+ conventions.
Use topic channels for versions (NOT versions.yml file).
Include proper container interpolation with both Docker and Singularity URLs.
The container block should adapt based on spec.container_source:
  - If DOCKERFILE, GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML, GENERATED_FROM_REQS, or CONVERTED_FROM_SINGULARITY:
    Both docker and singularity point to the same image using "docker://" prefix for singularity.
    Add a comment: // Container built from repo — see Dockerfile in module directory
  - If BIOCONTAINERS:
    Standard quay.io / depot.galaxyproject.org dual URL pattern.
  - If STUB:
    Include TODO comments on both container lines.
Include ext.args support via task.ext.args ?: ''.
Include version capture command as a comment placeholder: // TODO: capture version

The template variables are: spec (ModuleSpec object)

--- TEMPLATE 2: meta.yml.j2 ---
Generate a valid nf-core meta.yml with:
- name, description, keywords
- tools section with name, description, homepage, documentation, licence
- input section (from spec.inputs, each with type, description, pattern, ontologies)
- output section (from spec.outputs, same structure)
- authors list with placeholder
- maintainers list with placeholder
EDAM ontology: use http://edamontology.org/format_1930 for FASTQ,
               http://edamontology.org/format_2572 for BAM,
               http://edamontology.org/format_3003 for BED,
               http://edamontology.org/format_3475 for TSV
               Leave blank with TODO if unknown.

--- TEMPLATE 3: environment.yml.j2 ---
Generate a conda environment.yml with:
- name: nf-core-{tool_name}
- channels: [conda-forge, bioconda, defaults] (in that order — critical for nf-core)
- dependencies: [{spec.conda_package}]
If conda_package is a placeholder (contains "TODO"), add a YAML comment explaining it.
If spec.container_source is GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML, copy the source environment.yml
content verbatim rather than generating a new one.

--- TEMPLATE 4: Dockerfile.j2 ---
A flexible template used by container.py for GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML and GENERATED_FROM_REQS.
Template variables: source (Literal["envyml","reqs"]), tool_name, env_name,
                    conda_packages, pip_packages, python_version

For source=="envyml":
FROM condaforge/mambaforge:latest
LABEL maintainer="code-to-module <https://github.com/your-org/code-to-module>"
COPY environment.yml /tmp/environment.yml
RUN mamba env create -f /tmp/environment.yml && \
    mamba clean --all --yes && \
    conda clean --all --yes
ENV PATH /opt/conda/envs/{{ env_name }}/bin:$PATH

For source=="reqs":
FROM python:{{ python_version }}-slim
LABEL maintainer="code-to-module <https://github.com/your-org/code-to-module>"
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

--- TEMPLATE 5: tests/main.nf.test.j2 ---
Generate a valid nf-test file following nf-core testing conventions.
Include:
- nextflow_version check
- process import
- One test case per TestCase in spec.test_cases
- snapshot assertions for each expected output
- process_tags matching the label (process_single, etc.)
- config block pointing to nextflow.config
- For TestCase.is_stub_test == True: wrap entire test in `options "-stub"` block
- For CHAINED inputs: render a setup {} block before the when block that runs
  the upstream nf-core module named in TestCase.setup_module, then passes its
  output channel as this module's input
- For DERIVED inputs: reference `${projectDir}/tests/data/{filename}` — the file
  was produced by the derivation script and should be committed to nf-core/test-datasets
- For STUB inputs: insert a TODO comment block in the input section:
  // TODO: Replace this stub with real test data.
  // This module uses Nextflow stub mode — check main.nf stub: block.
  // To add proper test data, see: https://nf-co.re/docs/tutorials/tests_and_test_data/test_data
  // Options in priority order:
  //   1. Reuse an existing file from nf-core/test-datasets modules branch
  //   2. Derive from an existing nf-core file using a transformation script
  //   3. Chain an upstream nf-core module in a setup {} block
  //   4. Use stub: mode if data is too large or requires a local database

Example setup block for CHAINED inputs (template fragment):
{% if test_case.setup_module %}
    setup {
        run("{{ test_case.setup_module_process_name }}") {
            script "{{ test_case.setup_module }}"
            process {
                """
                input[0] = [
                    [ id:'test', single_end:false ],
                    file(params.modules_testdata_base_path + '{{ test_case.setup_module_input }}',
                         checkIfExists: true)
                ]
                """
            }
        }
    }
    when {
        process {
            """
            input[0] = {{ test_case.setup_module_process_name }}.out.{{ test_case.setup_module_output }}
            """
        }
    }
{% endif %}

--- TEMPLATE 6: derive_test_data/derive_vcf.sh.j2 ---
Create templates/derive_test_data/derive_vcf.sh.j2:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Auto-generated by code-to-module
# Derives a small test VCF by subsetting an existing nf-core/test-datasets VCF.
# Source: nf-core/test-datasets sarscov2 VCF
# Requires: bcftools
# After running: PR the output file to nf-core/test-datasets modules branch.
set -euo pipefail
OUTDIR="${1:-tests/data}"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"
SOURCE_VCF="{{ source_vcf }}"
bcftools view \
    --regions "{{ region | default('MN908947.3:1-5000') }}" \
    --output-type z \
    --output "$OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}.vcf.gz" \
    "$SOURCE_VCF"
bcftools index --tbi "$OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}.vcf.gz"
echo "Done. PR this file to nf-core/test-datasets modules branch:"
echo "  $OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}.vcf.gz"

--- TEMPLATE 7: derive_test_data/derive_fasta.sh.j2 ---
Create templates/derive_test_data/derive_fasta.sh.j2:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Auto-generated by code-to-module
# Derives a small test FASTA by extracting a region from an existing nf-core reference.
# Source: nf-core/test-datasets sarscov2 genome
# Requires: samtools
# After running: PR the output file to nf-core/test-datasets modules branch.
set -euo pipefail
OUTDIR="${1:-tests/data}"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"
SOURCE_FASTA="{{ source_fasta }}"
samtools faidx "$SOURCE_FASTA" \
    "{{ region | default('MN908947.3:1-5000') }}" \
    > "$OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}.fasta"
samtools faidx "$OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}.fasta"
echo "Done. PR this file to nf-core/test-datasets modules branch:"
echo "  $OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}.fasta"

--- TEMPLATE 8: derive_test_data/derive_bam.sh.j2 ---
Create templates/derive_test_data/derive_bam.sh.j2:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Auto-generated by code-to-module
# Derives a small test BAM by subsampling an existing nf-core/test-datasets BAM.
# Source: nf-core/test-datasets sarscov2 BAM
# Requires: samtools
# After running: PR the output file to nf-core/test-datasets modules branch.
set -euo pipefail
OUTDIR="${1:-tests/data}"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"
SOURCE_BAM="{{ source_bam }}"
samtools view -s 42.{{ subsample_pct | default(10) }} -b "$SOURCE_BAM" \
    > "$OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}.bam"
samtools sort -o "$OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}_sorted.bam" \
    "$OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}.bam"
samtools index "$OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}_sorted.bam"
echo "Done. PR these files to nf-core/test-datasets modules branch:"
echo "  $OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}_sorted.bam"
echo "  $OUTDIR/{{ sample_id | default('test') }}_sorted.bam.bai"

Note: there is NO template for generating FASTQ from scratch (no wgsim).
If a module requires FASTQ input, Strategy 1 (match) will always find the
sarscov2 paired or single-end FASTQ files in nf-core/test-datasets, which are
real subsampled reads. Only use derivation scripts for formats not already present
in nf-core/test-datasets.

After creating all templates, implement src/code_to_module/generate.py:
  def generate(
      spec: ModuleSpec,
      test_spec: TestSpec,
      outdir: Path,
      existing_modules: list[ExistingModule] | None = None,
  ) -> list[Path]
  Renders all module templates, writes files to outdir/{tool_name}/.
  After writing all files, if existing_modules is non-empty, run a
  consistency check: compare spec.container_docker and spec.label against
  each ExistingModule with load_error=None.
  For each divergence found, print a Rich warning panel (yellow, not red —
  this is advisory, not a blocking error):
    ⚠ Consistency warning: container differs from sibling module
      New module:     quay.io/biocontainers/liana:0.1.0--pyhdfd78af_0
      LIANA_BIVARIATE: quay.io/biocontainers/liana:0.0.6--pyhdfd78af_1
      Review: the new module may need an updated container version, or
      the sibling module may need updating. Check both before submitting.
  Label divergence warning:
    ⚠ Consistency warning: label differs from sibling module
      New module: process_high  •  LIANA_BIVARIATE: process_medium
      Review: confirm the resource requirements are intentionally different.
  These warnings do NOT fail the command or change the generated files.
  They are informational prompts for human review before nf-core submission.
  If spec.dockerfile_content is not None: write Dockerfile to outdir/{tool_name}/
  If test_spec.needs_derivation is True:
    Write test_spec.derivation_script_content to outdir/{tool_name}/tests/derive_test_data.sh
    Name it derive_test_data.sh (not generate_test_data.sh)
    Make it executable (chmod +x)
    Create outdir/{tool_name}/tests/data/.gitkeep
    The script header must include a prominent notice:
      "# IMPORTANT: After running this script, the generated files should be submitted"
      "# to nf-core/test-datasets via a pull request. See:"
      "# https://nf-co.re/docs/tutorials/tests_and_test_data/test_data#adding-new-test-data"
  Returns list of all created paths.
  Use rich.progress for file-by-file progress display.

Tests in tests/test_generate.py:
  - test_generate_creates_files: ModuleSpec + TestSpec → all 5 files written to tmp_path
  - test_consistency_check_container_divergence: existing_modules has one module with
    different container URL → warning printed, files still written, no exception raised
  - test_consistency_check_label_divergence: existing_modules has different label →
    warning printed
  - test_consistency_check_matching: existing_modules container and label match spec →
    no warning printed
  - test_consistency_check_skipped_when_empty: existing_modules=[] → no check runs
  - test_consistency_check_load_error_skipped: ExistingModule with load_error set →
    skipped in consistency check, no crash
  All file I/O uses tmp_path fixture.

docs.title: Prompt 10 — Test Data Matching (Strategy 1)
docs.text:
Goal: For each input channel, find the best existing file in nf-core/test-datasets using the rich index in the standards schema.

Implement src/code_to_module/test_data_match.py.

Export:
  def match(channel: ChannelSpec, standards: Standards) -> TestDataMatch | None

Where TestDataMatch is a new Pydantic model:
  TestDataMatch(
      channel_name: str,
      paths: list[str],          ← relative paths under test_data.base_path
      full_urls: list[str],      ← base_path + path for each file
      source: TestDataSource,    ← always NFCORE_DATASETS
      size_kb: int,              ← total size of matched files
      notes: str | None,         ← e.g. "sarscov2, paired-end, 45KB"
  )

Matching algorithm:
1. If channel.test_data_strategy == "custom" or "unknown": return None immediately
   (don't waste time searching — caller will use stub)

2. Build a query from channel.format_tags:
   - Look up each tag in standards.test_data_index
   - Filter to entries whose tags are a superset of channel.format_tags
   - If multiple matches: prefer smallest total size
   - If paired_end in channel.format_tags: ensure matched entry has paired files

3. Special handling for common cases:
   - ["fastq", "paired_end"] → use test_1.fastq.gz + test_2.fastq.gz (two paths)
   - ["fastq", "single_end"] → use test.fastq.gz (one path)
   - ["bam"] + no paired_end → use test.bam + test.bam.bai (include index)
   - ["fasta"] → use genome.fasta + genome.fasta.fai
   - ["vcf"] or ["vcf_gz"] → use test.vcf.gz + test.vcf.gz.tbi

4. If no match found: return None

Update nf_core_standards.json test_data section to use the rich index format:
Replace the flat "mappings" dict with an "index" array of objects:

docs.title: Prompt 11 — Test Data Derivation, Chaining, and Stub (Strategies 2–4)
docs.text:
Goal: When Strategy 1 finds no existing match in nf-core/test-datasets, choose the best fallback: deriving a new file by transforming existing nf-core data, chaining an upstream nf-core module via an nf-test setup block, or falling back to Nextflow stub mode. Wgsim and other de-novo data generators are explicitly not used — the nf-core community requires new test data to be derived from files already in nf-core/test-datasets, not generated from scratch.

Create src/code_to_module/test_data_derive.py (new file — no rename needed;
the project scaffold in Prompt 1 created this as an empty stub under the name
test_data_derive.py already). Similarly, tests/test_test_data_derive.py is new.

--- PART 1: Implement test_data_derive.py ---

Export:
  def plan_derivation(
      channel: ChannelSpec,
      standards: Standards,
      tool_name: str,
  ) -> DerivationPlan

DerivationPlan is a new Pydantic model:
  DerivationPlan(
      channel_name: str,
      strategy: Literal["derive", "chain", "stub"],
      format_tag: str,                ← primary format being handled
      template_name: str | None,      ← derive_*.sh.j2 template name, or None
      template_vars: dict,            ← Jinja2 vars for derivation template
      output_files: list[str],        ← filenames in tests/data/ (empty for chain/stub)
      tool_requirements: list[str],   ← bioconda packages needed (empty for chain/stub)
      setup_module: str | None,       ← for chain: module path e.g.
                                      ←   "modules/nf-core/samtools/sort/main.nf"
      setup_process_name: str | None, ← for chain: e.g. "SAMTOOLS_SORT"
      setup_input_path: str | None,   ← for chain: test-datasets path used as setup input
      setup_output_channel: str | None, ← for chain: output channel name, e.g. "bam"
      source: TestDataSource,         ← DERIVED | CHAINED | STUB
      notes: str,                     ← shown in summary table
      pr_instructions: str | None,    ← for DERIVED only: message about PRing to test-datasets
  )

--- PART 2: Strategy selection logic ---

plan_derivation() ALWAYS returns a DerivationPlan (never None).
Strategy priority order — use the FIRST that applies:

  1. DERIVE: if any entry in standards.derivation_templates has applicable_tags
     that intersects channel.format_tags, AND channel.test_data_strategy != "custom",
     AND the estimated output file size is <= 50MB.
     Derive is the preferred fallback over chain for most formats. A stored derived
     file keeps CI test runtime low, which is nf-core's stated priority. The derive
     script needs to be run once and the output PRed to nf-core/test-datasets, but
     that cost is paid once; every subsequent CI run is fast.

  2. CHAIN: if any entry in standards.chain_modules has produces_tags that is a
     superset of channel.format_tags, AND either:
     (a) the input file is estimated to be too large for nf-core/test-datasets (>50MB), OR
     (b) the chain_module entry has fast: true in the standards schema (meaning the
         upstream process completes in seconds — e.g. index generation from a small
         reference).
     Chain MUST NOT be used as a general convenience to avoid PRing test data.
     nf-core explicitly discourages setup{} blocks as a default because they increase
     test runtime — two processes run instead of one on every CI invocation.

     What "chain" means in practice: the generated nf-test runs a real upstream
     nf-core module inside a setup {} block to produce the input file on the fly,
     rather than reading it from disk. The canonical nf-core example is
     ABRICATE_SUMMARY, which requires ABRICATE_RUN to produce its report input.
     The setup block feeds a raw genome FASTA from nf-core/test-datasets into
     ABRICATE_RUN, whose report output flows directly into ABRICATE_SUMMARY's
     input channel. No intermediate file is stored. The same pattern applies to
     index files (BWA_INDEX, SAMTOOLS_FAIDX) where storing the index would be
     wasteful but re-generating it from a small FASTA is very fast.
     The trade-off: the test is slower (two processes instead of one) and requires
     the upstream module to be installed and passing in the test environment.

  3. STUB: all other cases, including:
     - channel.test_data_strategy == "custom" or "unknown"
     - format too large for GitHub and no chain module available
     - no matching derivation template and no chain module

Add a fast: bool field to each entry in standards.chain_modules:
  "samtools_faidx": { ..., "fast": true }   ← index from small FASTA: ~1 second
  "bwa_index":      { ..., "fast": true }   ← index from small FASTA: ~2 seconds
  "samtools_sort":  { ..., "fast": false }  ← sorting a BAM: slower, derive preferred

This field drives condition (b) in the CHAIN check above.

--- PART 3: Standards schema additions ---

Add to nf_core_standards.json under "test_data":

  "derivation_templates": {
    "derive_vcf": {
      "template": "derive_vcf.sh.j2",
      "tool_requirements": ["bcftools"],
      "applicable_tags": ["vcf", "vcf_gz", "bcf"],
      "source_id": "sarscov2_vcf",
      "output_pattern": "{sample_id}.vcf.gz"
    },
    "derive_fasta": {
      "template": "derive_fasta.sh.j2",
      "tool_requirements": ["samtools"],
      "applicable_tags": ["fasta", "fasta_gz"],
      "source_id": "sarscov2_fasta",
      "output_pattern": "{sample_id}.fasta"
    },
    "derive_bam": {
      "template": "derive_bam.sh.j2",
      "tool_requirements": ["samtools"],
      "applicable_tags": ["bam", "cram", "sam"],
      "source_id": "sarscov2_bam",
      "output_pattern": "{sample_id}_sorted.bam"
    },
    "derive_bed": {
      "template": "derive_bed.sh.j2",
      "tool_requirements": [],
      "applicable_tags": ["bed"],
      "source_id": "sarscov2_bed",
      "output_pattern": "{sample_id}.bed"
    }
  },

  "chain_modules": {
    "bwa_index": {
      "module": "modules/nf-core/bwa/index/main.nf",
      "process_name": "BWA_INDEX",
      "test_input_path": "genomics/sarscov2/genome/genome.fasta",
      "output_channel": "index",
      "produces_tags": ["bwa_index", "genome_index"],
      "fast": true
    },
    "samtools_faidx": {
      "module": "modules/nf-core/samtools/faidx/main.nf",
      "process_name": "SAMTOOLS_FAIDX",
      "test_input_path": "genomics/sarscov2/genome/genome.fasta",
      "output_channel": "fai",
      "produces_tags": ["fasta_index", "fai"],
      "fast": true
    },
    "samtools_sort": {
      "module": "modules/nf-core/samtools/sort/main.nf",
      "process_name": "SAMTOOLS_SORT",
      "test_input_path": "genomics/sarscov2/illumina/bam/test.bam",
      "output_channel": "bam",
      "produces_tags": ["bam", "sorted_bam"],
      "fast": false
    }
  }

Remove the old "generation_tools" key from nf_core_standards.json entirely.

--- PART 4: Standards schema sync and accessor verification ---

First, open src/code_to_module/standards/data/nf_core_standards.json and verify
that the chain_modules entries have a fast: bool field. If any entry is missing
it, add it now before touching any Python code:
  "bwa_index":      { ..., "fast": true }
  "samtools_faidx": { ..., "fast": true }
  "samtools_sort":  { ..., "fast": false }

This field is required by the strategy selection logic in Part 2. If it is absent
from the JSON, the _is_chainable() helper will raise a KeyError at runtime.

The four accessors below were already defined in Prompt 6. Verify they are
present and correct — do not add them again:
  derivation_templates, derivation_template_for(),
  chain_modules, chain_module_for()

chain_module_for() returns the full entry dict (including fast), so callers can
inspect entry["fast"] directly — no change to its signature is needed.

Remove the old generation_tools and generation_tool_for() accessors if they
were created as stubs during Prompt 1 scaffolding.

--- PART 5: Update test_gen.py orchestrator ---

Update src/code_to_module/test_gen.py to use the new three-strategy system.
Function signature is unchanged:
  def generate_test_spec(spec: ModuleSpec, source: CodeSource, standards: Standards) -> TestSpec

For each input channel in spec.inputs (skip meta and map types):
  1. Try Strategy 1: match(channel, standards) → TestDataMatch | None
  2. If None: plan_derivation(channel, standards, spec.tool_name) → DerivationPlan
     (always returns something — never None)

Build TWO TestCase objects:
  "test_{tool_name}" — the real test:
    NFCORE_DATASETS: full_urls from TestDataMatch
    DERIVED: "${projectDir}/tests/data/{filename}"; populate derivation_script
    CHAINED: leave input_files empty; populate setup_module, setup_process_name,
             setup_input_path, setup_output_channel from DerivationPlan
    STUB: leave input_files empty; mark in test with TODO comment block

  "test_{tool_name}_stub" — always present, options "-stub":
    Use NFCORE_DATASETS paths where available, else empty inputs
    This test always passes regardless of data strategy

Collect all DERIVED plans.
If any exist:
  Render a single derive_test_data.sh with this mandatory header:
    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    # Generated by code-to-module
    # Run this script ONCE to produce derived test data files.
    # IMPORTANT: The generated files must be submitted to nf-core/test-datasets:
    #   1. Fork https://github.com/nf-core/test-datasets
    #   2. Add files to the modules branch under the appropriate organism directory
    #   3. Open a PR against the modules branch
    #   4. Ask on the nf-core Slack #test-data channel before submitting
    # See: https://nf-co.re/docs/tutorials/tests_and_test_data/test_data#adding-new-test-data
    set -euo pipefail
  Then append each plan's rendered shell snippet.
  Set needs_derivation=True, derivation_script_content=combined script.

Print Rich summary table after resolving all channels:
  Channel  | Format       | Strategy | Notes
  reads    | fastq        | match    | sarscov2 paired-end FASTQ (90KB) — existing
  ref      | fasta        | match    | sarscov2 genome (35KB) — existing
  index    | bwa_index    | chain    | BWA_INDEX setup{} — fast upstream, no file needed
  annot    | vcf_gz       | derive   | subset sarscov2 VCF → PR to nf-core/test-datasets
  matrix   | count_matrix | stub     | stub: mode — no nf-core test data available

If any DERIVED plans exist, print a warning panel:
  "Action required: derived test data"
  "Run tests/derive_test_data.sh, then PR the output files to nf-core/test-datasets."
  "Always ask on Slack #test-data before submitting new files."

If any STUB plans exist, print a separate info panel:
  "Stub mode: some inputs have no available test data"
  "The module uses Nextflow stub: mode for these channels."
  "See TODO comments in tests/main.nf.test for guidance on adding data later."

--- PART 6: Tests in tests/test_test_data_derive.py ---

  - test_vcf_derives: ChannelSpec format_tags=["vcf_gz"], strategy="generatable"
    → DerivationPlan with strategy="derive", template="derive_vcf.sh.j2"
  - test_sorted_bam_derives: format_tags=["sorted_bam"], strategy="generatable"
    → DerivationPlan with strategy="derive" (samtools_sort is fast=false; derive_bam template applies)
  - test_bam_derives_not_chains: format_tags=["bam"] matches both
    chain_modules (samtools_sort, fast=false) and derivation_templates (derive_bam)
    → derive wins because samtools_sort is not fast: true and file is storable
  - test_fai_chains_not_derives: format_tags=["fai"] matches chain_modules
    (samtools_faidx, fast=true) but not derivation_templates
    → chain wins because upstream is fast and no derive template exists
  - test_custom_is_stub: channel.test_data_strategy="custom"
    → DerivationPlan with strategy="stub"
  - test_unknown_format_is_stub: format_tags=["count_matrix"] not in any template
    → strategy="stub"
  - test_rendered_derive_script_starts_with_shebang: shell content starts with
    "#!/usr/bin/env bash"
  - test_rendered_derive_script_has_pr_notice: script content contains
    "nf-core/test-datasets"
  - test_orchestrator_all_match: all channels have Strategy 1 matches
    → needs_derivation=False, no derive script
  - test_orchestrator_chain: one channel uses chain strategy
    → TestCase.setup_module populated, needs_derivation=False (chain produces no files)
  - test_orchestrator_derive: one channel needs derivation
    → needs_derivation=True, derivation_script_content not None, file has PR notice
  - test_orchestrator_stub_channel: one channel is stub
    → TestCase.input_files empty for real test, is_stub_test=False for that TestCase
  - test_stub_test_case_always_present: every TestSpec always includes exactly
    one TestCase with is_stub_test=True regardless of data strategy
  - test_summary_table_printed: orchestrator prints Rich table with Strategy column
  All tests use mock Standards with minimal schema. No real HTTP calls or file I/O.

docs.title: Prompt 12 — Container Tests (Write First)
docs.text:
Goal: Write the complete test suites for container discovery and selection before implementing either. These tests define the contract that container.py must satisfy. Claude Code will implement against them in Prompts 9a and 9b.

Why tests first here: Container resolution is the most complex stateful component in the system — it has six check paths, a tier-aware default algorithm, interactive and non-interactive selection modes, caching, and fallback chains. Writing tests after the implementation tends to produce tests that mirror the implementation's assumptions rather than catching wrong ones. Writing them first forces the contract to be explicit.

Write tests/test_container_discover.py and tests/test_container_select.py in full.
Do NOT write container.py yet — the implementation files do not exist.
Use pytest.importorskip or Mock objects for the unimplemented module where needed,
but structure tests so they will run and FAIL (not error) against a stub implementation.

--- tests/test_container_discover.py ---

Setup: create a minimal ContainerDiscovery stub at the top of the test file so
tests can import and reference the expected return types. Use pytest fixtures to
build CodeSource objects from the fixture directories without invoking ingest().

Core behaviour tests:
  - test_all_checks_always_run:
    Even when Dockerfile is present, BioContainers must still be checked.
    Mock both Dockerfile present and Bioconda 200. Assert result contains BOTH
    DOCKERFILE and BIOCONTAINERS options. This catches the "early return" bug.

  - test_stub_always_last_option:
    For any input (even one with Dockerfile + BioContainers), STUB must be present
    in discovery.options. Assert source=STUB in options list.

  - test_stub_is_only_option_when_nothing_found:
    No Dockerfile, no environment.yml, no requirements.txt, Bioconda 404.
    Assert len(options) == 1 and options[0].source == STUB.

Default selection tests — these define the tier-aware priority order precisely:
  - test_tier1_with_biocontainers_default_is_biocontainers:
    tier=1, both DOCKERFILE and BIOCONTAINERS found.
    Assert options[0].source == BIOCONTAINERS (BioContainers beats Dockerfile at Tier 1).

  - test_tier2_with_biocontainers_default_is_biocontainers:
    tier=2 behaves same as tier=1 for default selection.

  - test_tier3_with_dockerfile_default_is_dockerfile:
    tier=3, both DOCKERFILE and BIOCONTAINERS found.
    Assert options[0].source == DOCKERFILE (Dockerfile beats BioContainers at Tier 3+).

  - test_tier3_without_dockerfile_biocontainers_is_default:
    tier=3, no Dockerfile, BIOCONTAINERS found.
    Assert options[0].source == BIOCONTAINERS (fall through to BioContainers).

  - test_tier3_envyml_default_when_no_dockerfile_no_bio:
    tier=3, environment.yml present, Bioconda 404, no Dockerfile.
    Assert options[0].source == GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML.

  - test_exactly_one_default:
    For any combination of options, assert exactly one option has is_default=True.
    Parametrize across: (tier=1, bio+docker), (tier=3, bio+docker), (tier=5, stub only).

Container URL format tests:
  - test_biocontainers_docker_url_format:
    BioContainers option docker_url starts with "quay.io/biocontainers/".

  - test_biocontainers_singularity_url_format:
    BioContainers singularity_url starts with "https://depot.galaxyproject.org/singularity/".

  - test_dockerfile_github_url_uses_ghcr:
    Source is GitHub URL → docker_url starts with "ghcr.io/".

  - test_dockerfile_local_url_uses_tool_name:
    Source is local path → docker_url starts with tool_name.

  - test_stub_url_contains_todo:
    STUB option docker_url contains "TODO".

Network and caching tests:
  - test_biocontainers_cache_second_call_no_http:
    Call discover_sync twice for same tool_name.
    Mock httpx — assert HTTP is called exactly once across both calls.

  - test_biocontainers_404_no_option_added:
    Mock anaconda.org returning 404.
    Assert BIOCONTAINERS not in [opt.source for opt in result.options].

  - test_biocontainers_network_timeout_no_crash:
    Mock httpx.TimeoutException on anaconda.org call.
    Assert function returns successfully (no exception), BIOCONTAINERS absent.

  - test_github_tags_api_failure_uses_latest:
    Mock GitHub tags API returning 404.
    Assert Dockerfile option still created with tag "latest".

Content tests:
  - test_envyml_option_has_dockerfile_content:
    GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML option has dockerfile_content that is not None and not empty.

  - test_envyml_dockerfile_content_contains_mamba:
    dockerfile_content for GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML contains "mamba" or "conda".

  - test_singularity_def_conversion_has_run_command:
    Singularity.def with %post section → CONVERTED_FROM_SINGULARITY option's
    dockerfile_content contains "RUN".

All httpx calls mocked with pytest-mock or unittest.mock.
Do not make real HTTP calls. Do not read from real fixture repos — build minimal
CodeSource objects inline using fixture data.

--- tests/test_container_select.py ---

Setup: build ContainerDiscovery objects directly in tests using helper functions,
without calling discover(). Define a make_discovery() helper that takes a list of
ContainerSource values and returns a ContainerDiscovery with those options, the
first having is_default=True.

Flag tests:
  - test_flag_dockerfile_returns_dockerfile:
    make_discovery([DOCKERFILE, BIOCONTAINERS, STUB]).
    select(discovery, container_flag="dockerfile", ...) → DOCKERFILE returned.

  - test_flag_biocontainers_returns_biocontainers:
    select(discovery, container_flag="biocontainers", ...) → BIOCONTAINERS returned.

  - test_flag_generate_returns_envyml_when_available:
    make_discovery([GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML, STUB]).
    container_flag="generate" → GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML returned.

  - test_flag_generate_falls_back_to_reqs:
    make_discovery([GENERATED_FROM_REQS, STUB]).
    container_flag="generate" → GENERATED_FROM_REQS returned.

  - test_flag_stub_returns_stub:
    container_flag="stub" → STUB returned regardless of other options.

  - test_flag_missing_option_falls_through_to_default:
    make_discovery([BIOCONTAINERS, STUB]) (no DOCKERFILE).
    container_flag="dockerfile" → warning printed, default returned (BIOCONTAINERS).
    Assert warning message lists available options.

  - test_flag_missing_option_warning_lists_available:
    Same as above — assert warning text contains "biocontainers" and "stub".

Non-interactive tests:
  - test_no_interaction_returns_default_option:
    no_interaction=True → returns options[0] (the default).

  - test_no_interaction_prints_single_line:
    Capture console output — exactly one line printed, contains option label.

  - test_non_tty_returns_default:
    Mock sys.stdin.isatty() = False → returns default, no menu rendered.

  - test_non_tty_same_as_no_interaction:
    isatty=False with no_interaction=False → same result as no_interaction=True.

Interactive tests — mock click.prompt or sys.stdin:
  - test_interactive_valid_choice:
    Mock input returning "2" → second option returned.

  - test_interactive_valid_choice_first:
    Mock input returning "1" → first option returned.

  - test_interactive_empty_input_uses_default:
    Mock input returning "" → default (options[0]) returned.

  - test_interactive_invalid_input_reprompts:
    Mock input returning "99" then "1" → re-prompted once, first option returned.

  - test_interactive_invalid_twice_uses_default:
    Mock input returning "99" then "99" → default returned after two invalid inputs.

  - test_single_stub_only_skips_menu:
    make_discovery([STUB]) (only one option).
    Interactive mode → no menu shown, STUB returned with a warning.

  - test_interactive_shows_star_for_default:
    Capture console output — line containing default option includes "★" or "*".

  - test_interactive_shows_docker_url:
    Capture console output — docker_url (or truncated form) appears in menu.

  - test_interactive_shows_warning_for_option:
    Option with warnings — first warning appears in menu output.

Post-selection tests:
  - test_discovery_selected_field_updated:
    After select() returns, discovery.selected == returned option.

  - test_confirmation_line_printed:
    After selection, console output contains "Selected:" and the option label.

  - test_warnings_printed_after_selection:
    Option has non-empty warnings list → each warning printed after confirmation.

Use a null/captured Rich Console in all tests — never write to real stdout.
No real TTY interaction in any test.

docs.title: Prompt 13 — Container Discovery
docs.text:
Goal: Run all container checks in parallel and return every available option — never stop early.

Implement the discovery phase in src/code_to_module/container.py.

Export:
  async def discover(source: CodeSource, tool_name: str, tier: int) -> ContainerDiscovery

This function runs ALL checks concurrently using asyncio.gather() and collects
every available ContainerOption. It never stops at the first success — the user
will choose from all available options in the next phase.

--- CHECK 1: Dockerfile in repo ---
If source.container_hint.has_dockerfile is True:
  Read the Dockerfile content from source.container_hint.dockerfile_path.
  Extract the FROM line → base_image.
  Determine image tag:
    If source is a Git GitHub URL: GET https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/tags
    Use tags[0].name if available, else "latest".
    If local: "latest".
  Construct docker_url:
    GitHub URL: ghcr.io/{owner}/{repo}:{tag}
    Local: {tool_name}:{tag}
  singularity_url = "docker://{docker_url}"
  warnings = ["Build and push before nf-core submission: docker build -t {docker_url} ."]
  Yield ContainerOption(source=DOCKERFILE, label="Dockerfile in repo", ...)

--- CHECK 2: environment.yml in repo ---
If source.container_hint.has_environment_yml is True:
  Extract env_name from environment.yml `name:` field (default: tool_name).
  docker_url = "{tool_name}:latest" (or "ghcr.io/{owner}/{tool_name}:latest" for GitHub)
  singularity_url = "docker://{docker_url}"
  Render Dockerfile.j2 with source="envyml" → dockerfile_content
  warnings = ["Dockerfile generated from environment.yml — review, build, and push"]
  Yield ContainerOption(source=GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML, label="Generate from environment.yml", ...)

--- CHECK 3: requirements.txt in repo ---
If source.container_hint.has_requirements_txt is True:
  Detect Python version (scan for python_requires, default "3.10").
  docker_url = "{tool_name}:latest"
  singularity_url = "docker://{docker_url}"
  Render Dockerfile.j2 with source="reqs" → dockerfile_content
  warnings = ["Dockerfile generated from requirements.txt — review, build, and push"]
  Yield ContainerOption(source=GENERATED_FROM_REQS, label="Generate from requirements.txt", ...)

--- CHECK 4: Singularity.def in repo ---
If source.container_hint.has_singularity_def is True:
  Parse Bootstrap and From fields from .def file.
  Best-effort Dockerfile conversion (Bootstrap docker → use From as base, else ubuntu:22.04).
  Convert %post → RUN commands.
  warnings = ["Converted from Singularity.def — manual review strongly recommended",
              "%environment and %runscript sections not automatically converted"]
  Yield ContainerOption(source=CONVERTED_FROM_SINGULARITY,
                        label="Convert from Singularity.def", ...)

--- CHECK 5: BioContainers lookup ---
Always run this check regardless of what files were found in the repo.
  GET https://api.anaconda.org/package/bioconda/{tool_name}
  If 200: extract latest_version
    GET https://api.anaconda.org/package/bioconda/{tool_name}/files
    Find most recent linux-64 .conda or .tar.bz2, extract build string.
    docker_url = "quay.io/biocontainers/{tool_name}:{version}--{build_string}"
    singularity_url = "https://depot.galaxyproject.org/singularity/{tool_name}:{version}--{build_string}"
    Yield ContainerOption(source=BIOCONTAINERS, label="BioContainers", ...)
  If 404: skip (no option added for this source)

--- CHECK 6: Stub (always added as last resort) ---
Always add a stub option:
  docker_url = "quay.io/biocontainers/{tool_name}:TODO--TODO"
  singularity_url = "https://depot.galaxyproject.org/singularity/{tool_name}:TODO--TODO"
  warnings = ["No container — options:\n"
              "  1. Add a Dockerfile to your repo and re-run\n"
              "  2. Submit tool to Bioconda: https://bioconda.github.io/contributor/guidelines.html\n"
              "  3. Fill in container URLs manually before nf-core submission"]
  Yield ContainerOption(source=STUB, label="Stub (fill in manually)", ...)

--- Default flag assignment (Tier-aware) ---
After collecting all options, set is_default=True on exactly one option:
  If tier <= 2 (known tool) AND BioContainers option exists → BioContainers is default
  Else if DOCKERFILE option exists → DOCKERFILE is default
  Else if GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML exists → that is default
  Else if GENERATED_FROM_REQS exists → that is default
  Else if CONVERTED_FROM_SINGULARITY exists → that is default
  Else → STUB is default

Order options list: default option first, then rest in discovery order.

Use httpx.AsyncClient with timeout=10s, follow_redirects=True.
Cache BioContainers results: _biocontainers_cache: dict[str, ContainerOption | None] = {}

Add sync wrapper: def discover_sync(source, tool_name, tier) -> ContainerDiscovery

Run the tests written in Prompt 12:
  pytest tests/test_container_discover.py -v
All tests must pass before proceeding to Prompt 14.
Iterate on the implementation until they do — do not move on with failing tests.

docs.title: Prompt 14 — Container Selection
docs.text:
Goal: Present discovered options to the user and return their chosen ContainerOption.

Implement the selection phase in src/code_to_module/container.py.

Export:
  def select(
      discovery: ContainerDiscovery,
      container_flag: str | None,    ← value of --container CLI flag
      no_interaction: bool,          ← value of --no-interaction flag
      console: rich.console.Console,
  ) -> ContainerOption

Behaviour:

--- Case 1: --container flag provided ---
Map flag value to ContainerSource:
  "dockerfile"    → DOCKERFILE
  "biocontainers" → BIOCONTAINERS
  "generate"      → GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML or GENERATED_FROM_REQS (first available)
  "stub"          → STUB
Find the matching option in discovery.options.
If not found: print a Rich warning explaining which options ARE available,
  fall through to interactive or default selection.
Return the matched ContainerOption immediately (no menu shown).

--- Case 2: --no-interaction flag or non-TTY ---
Detect TTY: import sys; is_tty = sys.stdin.isatty()
If no_interaction=True OR not is_tty:
  Return discovery.options[0] (the default, already first in list).
  Print single line: "📦 Using [option label] (default)"

--- Case 3: Interactive (TTY, no flags) ---
If only one option available (just STUB): skip menu, return it with a warning message.
Otherwise render a Rich selection menu:

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  📦 Container options for {tool_name}               │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  [1] ★ BioContainers          (default)             │
  │        quay.io/biocontainers/samtools:1.18--h...    │
  │                                                     │
  │  [2]   Dockerfile in repo                           │
  │        ghcr.io/lab/samtools-wrapper:latest          │
  │        ⚠ Build and push before nf-core submission   │
  │                                                     │
  │  [3]   Stub (fill in manually)                      │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Select [1]:

Rules for the menu:
  - ★ marks the default option
  - Show docker_url (truncated to 55 chars) under each option
  - Show first warning (if any) under the URL, prefixed with ⚠
  - Empty input → use default (option marked with ★)
  - Invalid input → re-prompt once, then use default
  - Options with dockerfile_content show a 🐳 icon

After selection: print confirmation line:
  "✓ Selected: {label} → {docker_url}"
  If option has warnings: print each warning with ⚠ prefix

Set discovery.selected = chosen option.
Return chosen ContainerOption.

Also export convenience function used by cli.py:
  def resolve(
      source: CodeSource,
      tool_name: str,
      tier: int,
      container_flag: str | None,
      no_interaction: bool,
      console: Console,
  ) -> ContainerOption:
    """Run discover_sync then select. One-call interface for cli.py."""
    discovery = discover_sync(source, tool_name, tier)
    return select(discovery, container_flag, no_interaction, console)

Run the tests written in Prompt 12:
  pytest tests/test_container_select.py -v
All tests must pass before proceeding to Prompt 17.
Iterate until they do — do not move on with failing tests.

docs.title: Prompt 15 — Wire the CLI
docs.text:
Goal: Connect all modules into the working convert command, including the new discovery and selection step. For multi-functionality repos, loop over each selected functionality and generate one module per.

Implement the full convert command in src/code_to_module/cli.py.

Add new CLI flags:
  --functionalities TEXT    Comma-separated functionality names to generate (e.g. "align,sort")
  --all-functionalities     Select all detected functionalities without prompting

The full command flow:

1. Print a Rich welcome panel: "code-to-module v{version}"
   Run standards staleness check (unless --no-update-check).

2. Call ingest(source, docs=docs_flag, existing_modules=existing_modules_flag) → CodeSource
   - docs_flag: list collected from --docs (empty list if not provided)
   - existing_modules_flag: list collected from --existing-modules (empty list if not provided)
   - On ValueError: print error, exit code 1
   - Print container hint summary:
     "📂 Repo scan: {n} files | Dockerfile ✓ | environment.yml ✓ | ..."
   - If any DocSource has fetch_error: print warning but continue
   - If any ExistingModule has load_error: print warning but continue

3. Call discover(code_source) → DiscoveryResult
   - Show Rich spinner: "Scanning for functionalities..."
   - Call select_functionalities(result, functionalities_flag,
       all_functionalities_flag, no_interaction, console) → DiscoveryResult
     (shows menu if ≥ 2 found and not non-interactive)
   - If result.selected is empty: print error "No functionalities selected", exit code 2

4. If --dry-run:
   Print "Would generate {n} module(s): {names}" and exit 0.

5. For each func in result.selected (show progress: "Processing 1/3: align"):

   5a. Call assess(func, code_source) → (tier, confidence, warnings)
       Print tier panel for this functionality.
       If tier == 5: print explanation, skip this functionality (continue loop).
       If --tier override: use that tier.

   5b. Call container.resolve(code_source, func.name, tier,
                              container_flag, no_interaction, console) → ContainerOption
       Container resolution is per-functionality (each may need a different image).
       For multi-script repos: each FunctionalitySpec.code_section may reference
       a different tool → different BioContainers image may be appropriate.
       Print container selection result.

   5c. Call infer_module_spec_sync(func, code_source, tier) → ModuleSpec
       Show spinner: "Inferring: {func.display_name}..."
       Update ModuleSpec with container info from ContainerOption.
       Print summary: process name, input count, output count.

   5d. Call generate_test_spec(module_spec, code_source, standards) → TestSpec
       Show spinner: "Resolving test data: {func.name}..."
       Print strategy summary table.
       Print generation warning if needed.

   5e. Determine output directory for this module:
       {outdir}/{func.name}/   (e.g. modules/align/, modules/sort/)
       If only one functionality selected: {outdir}/{module_spec.tool_name}/

   5f. Call generate(module_spec, test_spec, module_outdir,
                     existing_modules=code_source.existing_modules) → list[Path]
       Print each file as created.

6. After loop completes, print final Rich panel:
   - "✓ Generated {n} module(s) successfully!"
   - For each module: list its directory and file count
   - Combined next steps:
     "For each module:"
     "  1. Review main.nf — check channel types and version capture"
     If any DOCKERFILE/GENERATED: "  2. Build & push containers (see per-module README)"
     If any needs_derivation:
       "  3. Derive test data: bash {module}/tests/derive_test_data.sh"
       "     Then PR the generated files to nf-core/test-datasets (ask on #test-data Slack first)"
     If any STUB channels:
       "  4. Module uses stub: mode for some inputs — see TODO comments in tests/main.nf.test"
     "  4. code-to-module review {outdir}/{name}"
     "  5. code-to-module test {outdir}/{name}"
     "  6. code-to-module fix {outdir}/{name}   # if step 5 has failures"


--- Annotated terminal transcripts ---

The following transcripts define the exact output format for the convert command.
Implement the output to match these — pixel-perfect panel borders are not required,
but content, order, and the presence/absence of each section must match.

TRANSCRIPT A — Multi-functionality repo, interactive selection, partial Tier-5 skip:

  $ code-to-module convert https://github.com/lab/mytool --outdir modules/

  ╭──────────────────────────────────╮
  │  code-to-module v0.1.0           │
  ╰──────────────────────────────────╯
  ⚡ nf-core standards update available: v3.6.0
     Run: code-to-module update-standards

  📂 Repo scan: 4 files | Dockerfile ✓ | environment.yml ✗ | requirements.txt ✗

  ⠋ Scanning for functionalities...

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  🔍 Functionalities found in: https://github.com/lab/mytool        │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  [1] ✓ align         Map reads to a reference genome               │
  │        Inputs: reads (fastq), reference (fasta)                    │
  │        Outputs: alignments (bam)                                   │
  │        Confidence: 95%  Detection: CLI subcommand (@command)       │
  │                                                                    │
  │  [2] ✓ sort          Sort a BAM file by coordinate                 │
  │        Inputs: alignments (bam)   Outputs: sorted_alignments (bam) │
  │        Confidence: 92%  Detection: CLI subcommand (@command)       │
  │                                                                    │
  │  [3] ✓ index         Index a sorted BAM file                       │
  │        Inputs: sorted_alignments (bam)   Outputs: index (bai)     │
  │        Confidence: 88%  Detection: CLI subcommand (@command)       │
  │                                                                    │
  │  [4] ⚠ _internal     Logging and config utilities                  │
  │        Confidence: 28%  Detection: LLM inference                   │
  │        Not pre-selected — likely internal utility                  │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Select [1,2,3] (Enter to accept, or type new selection, 'all', 'q' to quit): 1,2

  → Will generate 2 module(s): align, sort

  ── Processing 1/2: align ────────────────────────────────────────────

  ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
  │  Tier 1 — Known tool, Bioconda package exists                        │
  │  Tool: align  Confidence: 95%  Detection: CLI subcommand (@command)  │
  ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
  📦 Using BioContainers (default for Tier 1)
  ✓ Selected: BioContainers → quay.io/biocontainers/bwa:0.7.17--h5bf99c6_8

  ⠋ Inferring: Map reads to a reference genome...
  → MYTOOL_ALIGN: 2 inputs, 2 outputs

  ⠋ Resolving test data: align...
  Channel  │ Format       │ Strategy │ Notes
  ─────────┼──────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────
  reads    │ fastq        │ match    │ sarscov2 paired-end FASTQ (90KB)
  ref      │ fasta        │ match    │ sarscov2 genome (35KB)
  bam      │ bam          │ match    │ sarscov2 BAM (120KB)
  versions │ —            │ —        │ (versions channel, no test data needed)

  ✓ modules/align/main.nf
  ✓ modules/align/meta.yml
  ✓ modules/align/environment.yml
  ✓ modules/align/tests/main.nf.test
  ✓ modules/align/tests/nextflow.config
  ✓ Quick lint passed

  ── Processing 2/2: sort ─────────────────────────────────────────────

  ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
  │  Tier 5 — Cannot generate reliably                                   │
  │  Tool: sort  Confidence: 31%                                         │
  │  Reason: no Bioconda entry, no Dockerfile, ambiguous I/O structure   │
  ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
  ⚠ Skipping sort — Tier 5 requires manual module creation.
    See: https://nf-co.re/docs/contributing/modules

  ── Final summary ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
  │  ✓ Generated 1 module  •  ⚠ Skipped 1 (Tier 5)                      │
  │                                                                      │
  │  modules/align/   (5 files)   BioContainers  Tier 1                 │
  │  sort             skipped — Tier 5 — create manually                 │
  │                                                                      │
  │  Next steps for each generated module:                               │
  │    1. Review main.nf — check channel types and version capture       │
  │    2. validate-module review modules/align                              │
  │    3. validate-module test modules/align                                │
  │    4. validate-module fix modules/align   # if step 3 has failures      │
  ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Notes on this transcript:
- "── Processing N/N: name ──" uses Rich's console.rule() — a full-width horizontal
  rule with centred text. This is the progress marker for each functionality in the loop.
- The tier panel (╭/╰) is printed once per functionality before container selection.
- Tier 5 skips container selection, inference, and generation — prints ⚠ and continues.
- Exit code is 0 for partial success (some generated, some skipped).
- Exit code is 1 only when ALL functionalities fail or an unrecoverable error occurs.
- The final summary panel lists ALL selected functionalities — generated and skipped.
- "Next steps" in the final panel uses validate-module (not code-to-module) for test/fix/review.
- Standards update notice only prints when a newer version is detected — suppress if up to date.

TRANSCRIPT B — Single functionality, non-interactive, --no-interaction:

  $ code-to-module convert script.py --outdir modules/ --no-interaction

  ╭──────────────────────────────────╮
  │  code-to-module v0.1.0           │
  ╰──────────────────────────────────╯
  📂 Repo scan: 1 file | Dockerfile ✗ | environment.yml ✗ | requirements.txt ✗

  ⠋ Scanning for functionalities...
  → Single functionality: script

  ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
  │  Tier 3 — Custom script, language and I/O clear                      │
  │  Tool: script  Confidence: 72%  Detection: Single script             │
  ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
  📦 Using Stub (fill in manually) (default — no Dockerfile or BioContainers found)

  ⠋ Inferring: Script...
  → SCRIPT: 2 inputs, 1 output

  ⠋ Resolving test data: script...
  Channel  │ Format       │ Strategy │ Notes
  ─────────┼──────────────┼──────────┼────────────────────────────────────────
  reads    │ fastq        │ match    │ sarscov2 paired-end FASTQ (90KB)
  output   │ custom       │ stub     │ stub: mode — proprietary format

  ╭─ Action required: derived test data ──────────────────────────────────╮
  │  stub: mode active for 1 channel.                                     │
  │  See TODO comments in tests/main.nf.test for guidance.               │
  ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

  ✓ modules/script/main.nf
  ✓ modules/script/meta.yml
  ✓ modules/script/environment.yml
  ✓ modules/script/tests/main.nf.test
  ✓ modules/script/tests/nextflow.config
  ⚠ quick lint: missing_container — container line contains TODO
    → Run with --container biocontainers or add a Dockerfile

  ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
  │  ✓ Generated 1 module                                                │
  │                                                                      │
  │  modules/script/   (5 files)   Stub  Tier 3                         │
  │                                                                      │
  │  Next steps:                                                         │
  │    1. Review main.nf — check channel types and version capture       │
  │    2. Fill in container URLs in main.nf (currently TODO)             │
  │    3. stub: mode active — see TODO comments in tests/main.nf.test    │

Notes on Transcript B:
- Non-interactive → no selection menu, no container menu.
- Quick lint failure prints as ⚠ warning (not error) when severity=warning,
  ✗ (red) when severity=error. Does not block exit — always exits 0 if file
  generation succeeded.
- "Fill in container URLs" next step only appears when container is STUB.
- "stub: mode active" next step only appears when any channel has stub strategy.
- Next step numbering is dynamic — steps only appear when relevant.

TRANSCRIPT C — --dry-run with multi-functionality repo:

  $ code-to-module convert https://github.com/lab/mytool --outdir modules/ --dry-run \
      --no-interaction

  ╭──────────────────────────────────╮
  │  code-to-module v0.1.0           │
  ╰──────────────────────────────────╯
  📂 Repo scan: 4 files | Dockerfile ✓ | environment.yml ✗ | requirements.txt ✗

  ⠋ Scanning for functionalities...
  → Auto-selected: align (confidence 95%)
  → Auto-selected: sort (confidence 92%)
  → Auto-selected: index (confidence 88%)

  Would generate 3 module(s): align, sort, index
  (dry run — no files written)

Notes on Transcript C:
- --dry-run stops immediately after selection, before assessment.
- It does NOT run infer, container discovery, or test data resolution.
- "Auto-selected" lines appear for each pre-selected functionality.
- Exit code 0.


Add commands to main group:
  assess-only SOURCE — ingest + discover + assess, print tier panels, exit.
  Accepts --json flag to print a JSON array instead of Rich panels:
  [
    {"name": "align", "tier": 1, "confidence": 0.95, "warnings": []},
    {"name": "sort",  "tier": 5, "confidence": 0.31, "warnings": ["no Bioconda entry"]}
  ]
  Exit code 0. Never writes any files.

  Annotated transcript for assess-only (interactive/Rich output):

    $ code-to-module assess-only https://github.com/lab/mytool

    📂 Repo scan: 4 files | Dockerfile ✓

    ⠋ Scanning for functionalities...

    ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
    │  Tier 1 — Known tool, Bioconda package exists                        │
    │  Tool: align  Confidence: 95%  Detection: CLI subcommand (@command)  │
    ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
