        "Run `code-to-module update-standards` to sync."
    )

@pytest.mark.network
def test_standards_version_field_present():
    """nf_core_standards.json has a schema_version field for change tracking."""
    from code_to_module.standards import get_standards
    standards = get_standards()
    assert hasattr(standards, "schema_version"), (
        "Add a schema_version field to nf_core_standards.json to enable "
        "drift tracking over time"
    )

All failures in this file are advisory — the tests assert things that should
trigger a manual update to standards, not block a release. Consider wrapping
the entire file in a try/except at the module level that converts assertion
errors to pytest.warns() rather than pytest.fails(), so a broken upstream
template doesn't block CI. Document the trade-off in a comment.

Run with:
  pytest -m network -v

docs.title: Prompt 28 — Regression Test Suite
docs.text:
Goal: Semantic comparison of code-to-module output against known-correct nf-core modules. Catches quality regressions in infer.py and generate.py that unit tests and invariant checks miss — the difference between a module that passes structural checks and one that a real nf-core reviewer would accept.

This is the most expensive test in the suite (real API calls + real nf-core clones). It belongs in pytest -m regression and should be run manually before releases, not in every CI run.

New files: tests/test_regression.py, src/code_to_module/regression.py.

Implement src/code_to_module/regression.py and tests/test_regression.py.

--- PART 1: regression.py ---

Export three things:

1. ParsedModule dataclass:
   @dataclass
   class ParsedModule:
       process_name: str           # e.g. "SAMTOOLS_SORT"
       container_docker: str       # quay.io/biocontainers/... URL
       input_names: list[str]      # emit names of input channels (excluding meta)
       output_names: list[str]     # emit names of output channels (excluding versions)
       has_versions_emit: bool
       has_ext_args: bool
       has_todo: bool
       script_tool_name: str       # first word of the tool invocation in script block
       keywords: list[str]         # from meta.yml keywords field

2. def parse_module(module_path: Path) -> ParsedModule
   Reads main.nf and meta.yml. Uses regex for main.nf parsing (not a full
   Nextflow parser — regex is sufficient for the fields above).
   Raises ValueError if required files are missing.

3. RegressionScore dataclass and semantic_score function:
   @dataclass
   class RegressionScore:
       process_name_match: bool     # exact match
       container_match: bool        # exact match on docker URL
       output_coverage: float       # fraction of reference outputs present in generated
       input_coverage: float        # fraction of reference inputs present in generated
       has_versions_emit: bool
       has_ext_args: bool
       no_todos: bool
       keyword_overlap: float       # Jaccard similarity of keyword sets
       overall: float               # weighted mean of above (see weights below)

   WEIGHTS = {
       "process_name_match": 0.20,
       "container_match":    0.20,
       "output_coverage":    0.20,
       "input_coverage":     0.10,
       "has_versions_emit":  0.10,
       "has_ext_args":       0.10,
       "no_todos":           0.05,
       "keyword_overlap":    0.05,
   }
   # weights sum to 1.0; process name and container are the highest-value signals

   def semantic_score(generated: ParsedModule, reference: ParsedModule) -> RegressionScore

--- PART 2: tests/test_regression.py ---

REFERENCE_MODULES = [
    {
        "id": "samtools_sort",
        "tool_url": "https://github.com/samtools/samtools",
        "docs_url": None,
        "reference_path": "modules/nf-core/samtools/sort",    # cloned from nf-core/modules
        "min_overall_score": 0.70,
        "must_match": ["process_name_match", "container_match"],
    },
    {
        "id": "fastqc",
        "tool_url": "https://github.com/s-andrews/FastQC",
        "docs_url": "https://www.bioinformatics.babraham.ac.uk/projects/fastqc/",
        "reference_path": "modules/nf-core/fastqc",
        "min_overall_score": 0.70,
        "must_match": ["process_name_match", "container_match", "has_versions_emit"],
    },
    {
        "id": "cutadapt",
        "tool_url": "https://github.com/marcelm/cutadapt",
        "docs_url": "https://cutadapt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/",
        "reference_path": "modules/nf-core/cutadapt",
        "min_overall_score": 0.70,
        "must_match": ["process_name_match", "container_match"],
        # Replaced trimgalore (Perl, Tier 5 — discovery cannot handle Perl wrappers
        # reliably). cutadapt is Python, single-functionality, Bioconda-backed,
        # and passed the Stage 8 regression run cleanly.
    },
]

Session-scoped fixture: clone nf-core/modules once to a shared cache:
  git clone --depth=1 --filter=blob:none --sparse \
    https://github.com/nf-core/modules nfcore_modules_cache
  git sparse-checkout add modules/nf-core/samtools/sort
  git sparse-checkout add modules/nf-core/fastqc
  git sparse-checkout add modules/nf-core/cutadapt
(Sparse checkout keeps the clone fast — only the three target module directories
are downloaded.)

@pytest.mark.regression
@pytest.mark.llm
@pytest.mark.network
@pytest.mark.parametrize("target", REFERENCE_MODULES, ids=[t["id"] for t in REFERENCE_MODULES])
def test_regression_score(target, nfcore_cache, tmp_path):
    """Generated module meets minimum semantic score against nf-core reference."""
    convert(
        source=target["tool_url"],
        outdir=tmp_path,
        docs=[target["docs_url"]] if target["docs_url"] else [],
        no_interaction=True,
    )
    tool_name = target["id"].split("_")[0]
    generated = parse_module(tmp_path / tool_name)
    reference = parse_module(nfcore_cache / target["reference_path"])
    score = semantic_score(generated, reference)

    assert score.overall >= target["min_overall_score"], (
        f"{target['id']}: overall score {score.overall:.2f} below "
        f"minimum {target['min_overall_score']}\n"
        f"Scores: {score}"
    )
    for field in target["must_match"]:
        assert getattr(score, field), (
            f"{target['id']}: must-match field '{field}' failed\n"
            f"Scores: {score}"
        )

Also implement:

def test_parse_module_on_fixture():
    """parse_module works on the chain_input fixture from Prompt 23."""
    # Run convert with injected spec first (no LLM), then parse
    ...
    parsed = parse_module(module_path)
    assert parsed.process_name == "CHAINTOOL"
    assert parsed.has_versions_emit
    assert not parsed.has_todo

def test_semantic_score_identical_modules():
    """semantic_score returns overall=1.0 when comparing a module to itself."""
    parsed = parse_module(chain_module_path)
    score = semantic_score(parsed, parsed)
    assert score.overall == 1.0
    assert score.process_name_match
    assert score.output_coverage == 1.0

Run with:
  ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=... pytest -m regression -v

The minimum score thresholds (0.70, 0.65) are starting points. Lower them
only if a known-good generation consistently falls below them; raise them
as generation quality improves. Track threshold history in a comment in
test_regression.py so you can see quality trends over time.

docs.title: Prompt 29 — Full Test Run
docs.text:
Goal: Run every test stage in the order defined in CLAUDE.md's Testing Protocol, report results stage by stage, and confirm the tool is ready for real-world use. This is the "done" checkpoint for the tutorial — all 28 prompts are implemented and the full suite is green.

No new source files. This prompt is pure orchestration.

Run all test stages in the order below. Report each stage separately before
proceeding to the next. Do not batch results across stages.

--- STAGE 1: Unit suite (fast, no network, no LLM) ---

  pytest -x -q -m "not network and not llm"

Expected: all tests green. If anything is red, stop and fix before continuing.
Report the count: X passed, Y failed, Z skipped.

--- STAGE 2: Architectural boundary enforcement ---

  pytest tests/test_import_boundaries.py -v

Expected: all boundary tests green. A failure here means a module in the
validation suite (validate_cli.py, fix.py, review.py, standards/) has
imported from the conversion pipeline — fix the import before continuing.
Report each tested file and its result explicitly.

--- STAGE 3: End-to-end chain test ---

  pytest tests/test_chain.py -v

Expected: all chain tests green. These exercise the full convert→review
pipeline with an injected spec (no LLM call). A failure here means the
pipeline has a cross-module integration bug that unit tests didn't catch.
Report each test case and its result.

--- STAGE 4: Real-world integration — Celltypist ---

  code-to-module convert https://github.com/Teichlab/celltypist \
    --outdir /tmp/celltypist_final \
    --docs https://celltypist.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ \
    --no-interaction

Then check every item in the Prompt 16 manual checkpoint. Report each as
PASS or FAIL — do not summarise:

  [ ] Process named CELLTYPIST (not MAIN)
  [ ] Tier 2 assessment, confidence >= 80%
  [ ] BioContainers container URL present (not a stub URL)
  [ ] Container URL starts with quay.io/biocontainers/celltypist
  [ ] 6 inputs including meta
  [ ] gene_file marked optional: true
  [ ] cell_file marked optional: true
  [ ] No TODO in script block
  [ ] No duplicate emit: versions
  [ ] Stub touch commands match output globs (*.csv or *.h5ad, not channel names)
  [ ] task.ext.args present in script block
  [ ] versions.yml written in script block

If any checkpoint item is FAIL, identify the root cause and fix it in the
relevant source module (not in the generated output file). Re-run the
convert command and re-check the failing items after the fix.

--- STAGE 5: Discovery robustness (network required) ---

  pytest tests/test_discovery_robustness.py -v -m network

This clones real GitHub repos — it will take several minutes.
If a clone fails due to a transient network error, retry once.
Report each parametrized test case (celltypist, multiqc, pyfastx, etc.)
and its result. A failure here means the discovery pipeline has a parser
edge case that the synthetic fixtures didn't surface.

--- STAGE 6: Standards drift check (network required) ---

  pytest tests/test_standards_drift.py -v -m network

Failures here are advisory — they indicate the live nf-core template has
diverged from nf_core_standards.json. Report any failures and the specific
field that has drifted, but do not block on them if the unit suite is green.
Run code-to-module update-standards if any drift is detected.

--- STAGE 7: LLM contract tests (opt-in) ---

Only run this stage if ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set in the environment:

  pytest tests/test_llm_contract.py -v -m llm

For each invariant test, report whether the failure (if any) is in the raw
LLM output or in the post-processing guard in infer.py. These are different
fixes: a raw LLM failure may need a prompt improvement; a guard failure needs
a code fix in infer.py.

--- STAGE 8: Regression suite (opt-in, slow) ---

Only run this stage if ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set and you have time (expect
10–20 minutes for three reference modules):

  pytest tests/test_regression.py -v -m regression

Report the RegressionScore for each reference module (samtools/sort, fastqc,
cutadapt). If any module scores below its minimum threshold, identify which
dimension(s) failed (process_name, container_match, output_coverage, etc.)
and trace the failure to infer.py or generate.py.

--- FINAL REPORT ---

After all stages, produce a summary table:

  Stage | Result  | Notes
  ──────┼─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────
  1     | ✓ PASS  | 304 passed, 0 failed, 27 deselected
  2     | ✓ PASS  | All boundaries clean
  3     | ✓ PASS  | 4 chain tests passed
  4     | ✓ PASS* | 10/12 checkpoint items; 2 LLM-output variations (not defects)
  5     | ✓ PASS  | 13/13 (celltypist/cutadapt/multiqc/pyfastx)
  6     | ✓ PASS  | No schema drift detected
  7     | ✓ PASS  | 5/6 LLM invariants; 1 skipped (empty script_template — correct)
  8     | ✓ PASS  | samtools ✓, fastqc ✓, cutadapt ✓

If all stages pass: print "code-to-module is ready for real-world use."
If any mandatory stage (1–4) has unresolved failures: print the list of
failures and do not declare the tool ready.
Stages 5–8 failures are advisory — note them but do not block the declaration.

docs.title: Prompt 30 — README Update
docs.text:
Goal: Bring README.md in line with the real tool after real-world testing. The README written in Prompt 18 was a forward-looking spec; this prompt rewrites it to reflect actual CLI output, actual limitations, and the Celltypist example as a concrete worked illustration. It is the first thing a potential user or contributor reads on GitHub — it should be accurate and honest, not aspirational.

No new source files. This prompt edits README.md only.

Rewrite README.md. Read the following before writing anything:
  - All --help output from: code-to-module, code-to-module convert,
    code-to-module assess-only, code-to-module containers,
    code-to-module update-standards, validate-module, validate-module test,
    validate-module fix, validate-module review
  - The Overview section of docs/development-tutorial.md
  - The Prompt 16 manual checkpoint in docs/development-tutorial.md
    (use the Celltypist expected output as the worked example)
  - The Lessons Learned appendix in docs/development-tutorial.md
    (for the known limitations section)

Structure the README as follows. Use plain prose for descriptive sections,
code blocks and tables where they add clarity. Keep it under 600 lines.

--- SECTION 1: Header ---
One-sentence description. Badges: Python version, licence, CI status.

--- SECTION 2: Overview (150–200 words) ---
Draw from the Overview in docs/development-tutorial.md. Cover the problem
(blank-file module authorship), what the tool does, what "automated" means
honestly, and where it fits alongside nf-core modules create and Seqera AI.
Do not reproduce the tutorial verbatim — summarise and link.

--- SECTION 3: Quick start ---
Minimum viable workflow: install, one convert command, validate-module review.
Show the actual Celltypist terminal output from the Prompt 16 checkpoint
(process name, tier badge, container URL, input/output count). Truncate to fit;
do not invent output that doesn't match real behaviour.

--- SECTION 4: What gets generated ---
Output directory structure with a brief annotation of each file.

--- SECTION 5: Complexity tiers ---
The five-tier table with one-line descriptions. Note BioContainers default
for Tier 1–2, Dockerfile/generated default for Tier 3–5.

--- SECTION 6: Container strategies ---
The container strategy table. Brief note on Tier-aware default order.

--- SECTION 7: validate-module ---
Three short paragraphs: test, fix, review. Show the ReviewReport format.
Include Class A/B/C failure classification for fix. One key point:
fix always shows a diff and requires explicit approval before writing.

--- SECTION 8: Known limitations ---
This section must be honest. Cover:
- Tier 4–5 tools: Perl wrappers, no detectable CLI, database-dependent tools
- Library-only tools not yet supported (library-to-module is planned)
- Domain-specific test data (h5ad, pkl, mzML) falls back to stub
- LLM non-determinism: two runs may produce slightly different output
- The tool does not submit PRs — you review and submit yourself
Do not frame limitations as bugs or apologise for them.

--- SECTION 9: Requirements ---
Python 3.10+, nf-core >= 2.14, nf-test, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.
Mention the devcontainer option.

--- SECTION 10: Contributing ---
Two short paragraphs:
1. Contributing to code-to-module: standard fork/PR/test workflow;
   point to docs/development-tutorial.md for architectural context.
2. Contributing generated modules: pointer to nf-core modules guide
   at https://nf-co.re/docs/contributing/modules

--- SECTION 11: Licence ---
State the licence from pyproject.toml.

--- FORMATTING RULES ---
- No nested bullet lists — use prose or tables instead
- Every code block must have a language tag (bash, text, nextflow)
- Tone: practical and collaborative; assume the reader knows Nextflow
- Do not reproduce tutorial content verbatim — summarise and link

docs.title: Prompt 31 — MkDocs Documentation Site
docs.text:
Goal: Build a full documentation site using MkDocs with the Material theme — the standard in the nf-core ecosystem. Four pages: quick-start index, user guide with the Celltypist worked example, auto-generated CLI reference, known-limitations. The development tutorial is linked but not duplicated.

New files: mkdocs.yml, docs/index.md, docs/user-guide.md, docs/cli-reference.md, docs/known-limitations.md, docs/contributing.md, .github/workflows/docs.yml. docs/development-tutorial.md is not modified.

Set up a MkDocs documentation site. Read README.md and the --help output
for all commands before writing any content — the docs must match the
real CLI, not the spec.

--- PART 1: Dependencies ---

Add to pyproject.toml under [project.optional-dependencies]:
  docs = [
    "mkdocs>=1.6",
    "mkdocs-material>=9.5",
    "mkdocs-click>=0.8",
  ]

--- PART 2: mkdocs.yml ---

  site_name: code-to-module
  site_description: LLM-assisted nf-core module generation from scripts and repos
  repo_url: https://github.com/your-org/code-to-module
  repo_name: code-to-module
  edit_uri: edit/main/docs/

  theme:
    name: material
    palette:
      - scheme: default
        primary: teal
        accent: teal
        toggle:
          icon: material/brightness-7
          name: Switch to dark mode
      - scheme: slate
        primary: teal
        accent: teal
        toggle:
          icon: material/brightness-4
          name: Switch to light mode
    features:
      - navigation.tabs
      - navigation.sections
      - content.code.copy
      - content.code.annotate

  nav:
    - Home: index.md
    - User Guide: user-guide.md
    - CLI Reference: cli-reference.md
    - Known Limitations: known-limitations.md
    - Contributing: contributing.md
    - Development Tutorial: development-tutorial.md

  plugins:
    - search
    - mkdocs-click

  markdown_extensions:
    - admonition
    - pymdownx.details
    - pymdownx.superfences:
        custom_fences:
          - name: mermaid
            class: mermaid
            format: !!python/name:pymdownx.superfences.fence_code_format
    - pymdownx.highlight:
        anchor_linenums: true
    - pymdownx.tabbed:
        alternate_style: true
    - tables
    - attr_list

--- PART 3: docs/index.md ---

Home page content:
- One-paragraph description (from README Overview, not verbatim)
- Quick-start code block (install + convert + validate-module review)
- How it works diagram as a Mermaid flowchart:

  flowchart LR
    A[Script or repo] --> B[ingest]
    B --> C[discover]
    C --> D[assess tier]
    D --> E[infer with Claude]
    E --> F[generate module]
    F --> G[validate-module test]
    G --> H{failures?}
    H -- yes --> I[validate-module fix]
    I --> G
    H -- no --> J[validate-module review]
    J --> K[Submit PR to nf-core]

- Links to User Guide, CLI Reference, and Known Limitations
- An admonition block "When to use this tool" covering the Tier 1–2
  sweet spot and briefly listing what is not yet supported

--- PART 4: docs/user-guide.md ---

Four sections:

1. Installation — pip install, devcontainer option, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

2. Your first module — full Celltypist worked example:
   - The convert command with --docs
   - Annotated terminal output from the Prompt 16 checkpoint
     (tier badge, container URL, input/output count — use actual output,
     do not invent)
   - The generated file tree
   - Running validate-module review and interpreting the output
   - What to do next: run derive_test_data.sh if present, PR to
     nf-core/test-datasets, then submit the module PR

3. Test data strategies — plain-language explanation of match, derive,
   chain, and stub, with a table showing when each applies. Link to the
   nf-core test-datasets contribution guide for derive/chain cases.

4. The validate-module suite — test, fix, review with example output.
   Class A/B/C classification. Clarify that fix always shows a diff
   and requires explicit approval — it never silently modifies files.

--- PART 5: docs/cli-reference.md ---

  # CLI Reference

  ## code-to-module

  ::: mkdocs-click
      :module: code_to_module.cli
      :command: main
      :depth: 2
      :list_subcommands: true

  ## validate-module

  ::: mkdocs-click
      :module: code_to_module.validate_cli
      :command: main
      :depth: 2
      :list_subcommands: true

Before running mkdocs build, verify every Click command and option has
a non-empty help string. If any are missing, add them to cli.py or
validate_cli.py first. The CLI reference page is entirely auto-generated
— do not write manual content here.

--- PART 6: docs/known-limitations.md ---

Four admonition blocks:

  !!! warning "Tools that assess as Tier 4–5"
      Perl wrappers, tools with no detectable CLI structure, and tools
      that require large proprietary databases (BLAST, Kraken2) typically
      assess as Tier 4 or 5. The tool reports the tier honestly and
      generates what it can; Tier 5 tools require manual module authoring.

  !!! info "Library-only tools (no CLI entry point)"
      Tools like decoupler and liana-py expose a Python API but no
      command-line interface. These are not yet supported. Library-to-module
      support is planned — see the development tutorial for the proposed
      architecture.

  !!! info "Domain-specific test data"
      Formats not in nf-core/test-datasets (h5ad, pkl, mzML, Visium
      directories) fall back to stub mode. Real test data must be added
      manually and PRed to nf-core/test-datasets before submission.

  !!! note "LLM non-determinism"
      Running convert twice on the same tool may produce slightly different
      channel names, output globs, or script arguments. This is expected
      behaviour — always review the generated module before submitting.
      The post-processing guards in infer.py enforce structural invariants
      (meta as first input, no duplicate versions emit, no TODO placeholders)
      but do not guarantee identical output across runs.

End with a short "Planned improvements" subsection listing library-to-module,
Strategy 2c (test data from tool docs), and snakemake-to-nextflow as
known future directions. Link to GitHub Issues.

--- PART 7: docs/contributing.md ---

Two sections:

1. Contributing to code-to-module
   - Fork, branch, implement (follow prompt order in development tutorial),
     test with pytest -m "not network and not llm", open PR
   - Read docs/development-tutorial.md before contributing — it explains
     architectural decisions that aren't obvious from the code alone
   - Key rules: import boundary (validate suite must not import from
     conversion pipeline), fix-is-general principle (no special-case patches)

2. Contributing modules generated by this tool
   - Pointer to https://nf-co.re/docs/contributing/modules
   - Note the derive_test_data.sh → nf-core/test-datasets PR workflow
   - Mention the nf-core Slack #modules channel for review questions

--- PART 8: GitHub Actions deployment ---

Create .github/workflows/docs.yml:

  name: Deploy docs
  on:
    push:
      branches: [main]
      paths: ['docs/**', 'mkdocs.yml', 'src/**']
  permissions:
    contents: write
  jobs:
    deploy:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
          with:
            python-version: '3.11'
        - run: pip install -e ".[docs]"
        - run: mkdocs gh-deploy --force

Note: src/** is included in the trigger path because cli-reference.md
is generated from Click docstrings — a change to cli.py should redeploy
the docs.

--- PART 9: Verify ---

Run:
  pip install -e ".[docs]"
  mkdocs build --strict

Fix any warnings or errors before declaring done. Common issues to check:
- Missing help strings on Click options → add to cli.py / validate_cli.py
- Mermaid blocks not rendering → verify pymdownx.superfences custom_fences config
- mkdocs-click import errors → verify module paths in cli-reference.md match
  the actual module structure
- Broken internal links → mkdocs build --strict will catch these

Do not run mkdocs gh-deploy locally — CI handles deployment.
Report the final mkdocs build output (file count, any warnings).

docs.title: Quick Reference: Key nf-core 3.5+ Conventions
docs.text:
Note: These values are encoded in src/code_to_module/standards/data/nf_core_standards.json and accessed via standards.py. The snippets below are for human reference only — code should always read from get_standards(), never from this section. Run code-to-module update-standards to check for schema updates.

Topic channels (versions) — NEW in 3.5: 

output:
    tuple val(meta), path("*.bam"), emit: bam
    path "versions.yml"            , emit: versions, topic: 'versions'

Container block: 

conda "${moduleDir}/environment.yml"
container "${ workflow.containerEngine == 'singularity' && !task.ext.singularity_pull_docker_container ?
    'https://depot.galaxyproject.org/singularity/tool:1.0.0--h1234567_0' :
    'quay.io/biocontainers/tool:1.0.0--h1234567_0' }"

ext.args pattern: 

script:
def args = task.ext.args ?: ''
def prefix = task.ext.prefix ?: "${meta.id}"
"""
tool $args \\
    --input $reads \\
    --output ${prefix}.bam
"""

nf-test stub block: 

test("sarscov2 - bam - stub") {
    options "-stub"
    when { process { input[0] = [ [id:'test'], file(params.modules_testdata_base_path + '...') ] } }
    then { assertAll( { assert process.success }, { assert snapshot(process.out).match() } ) }
}

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Discovery found zero functionalities This happens when the source has no detectable CLI structure and the LLM also couldn't identify distinct operations. Common causes: the script is a helper/utility rather than a standalone tool, the file is too short to analyse, or the repo contains only config files. Try code-to-module assess-only SOURCE to see the Tier assessment, or pass the specific script file path rather than the whole repo directory.

Discovery found functionalities I don't want Use --functionalities name1,name2 to specify exactly which to generate, or run without --no-interaction to use the selection menu and deselect entries. Low-confidence entries (marked ⚠) are not pre-selected by default.

LLM detection produced wrong functionality boundaries LLM_INFERENCE fires only when no rule-based detector finds ≥ 2 functionalities. If the results look wrong, the rule-based detector may have missed the entry points: - click/typer: non-standard decorator wrappers can fool the AST detector - bash: the case statement detector requires $1, ${1}, CMD, or COMMAND as the dispatch variable; other patterns are missed Use --functionalities to name the parts you want explicitly, bypassing detection.

Multiple modules generated but process names conflict Each module gets a process name like TOOLNAME_SUBCOMMAND. Conflicts can occur with LLM-detected functionalities that produce ambiguous names. Solution: run with --functionalities to supply clearer names, or edit the process line in the generated main.nf directly before testing.

code-to-module test exits immediately: "nf-test not found" Install nf-test: conda install -c nf-core nf-test or follow https://www.nf-test.com/installation. The test command requires both nf-core (for lint) and nf-test on PATH.

code-to-module fix reports "LLM returned unparseable diff" The LLM proposed a fix but the response wasn't a valid unified diff. This fix has been automatically downgraded to Class C — apply it manually. This is most common when the module has unusual structure that doesn't match the narrow prompt the fix command uses.

code-to-module fix --yes didn't apply all fixes --yes only auto-approves Class A (rule-based) fixes. Class B (LLM-assisted) fixes always require interactive approval — they may be wrong and carry a lower trust signal. Run without --yes to review Class B fixes individually.

code-to-module review reports errors but nf-core lint passes review and nf-core lint check different things: review applies higher-level nf-core style conventions (EDAM ontology, channel naming, label appropriateness) that lint doesn't enforce. Fix review errors first, then re-run lint.

Class C failure: "MODULE_TEMPLATE_OUTDATED" Run: nf-core modules patch {module_name} — this is a human-only fix. code-to-module fix will not attempt it.

Class C failure: biological/logic error in nf-test output The module ran but produced wrong output. Review the script: block carefully — check that the tool's actual command-line interface matches what main.nf calls, and that output file naming matches the emit patterns. code-to-module review may flag related style issues that give hints about what's wrong.

nf-test fails: "No such file or directory" for test data If your module has DERIVED inputs, you need to run derive_test_data.sh first: 

conda install -c bioconda samtools bcftools  # tools used by the derivation script
bash tests/derive_test_data.sh
Files are written to tests/data/. Before submitting to nf-core, these files must be moved to nf-core/test-datasets — nf-core does not accept test data committed directly to the module repo.

Submitting derived test data to nf-core/test-datasets 1. Ask on the nf-core Slack #test-data channel before submitting any new files. 2. Fork https://github.com/nf-core/test-datasets and add your files to the modules branch under data/ in an appropriate organism/format subdirectory. 3. Name derived files after their upstream source: if derived from test.bam, call the output test.<new_extension>. 4. Add an entry to the branch README describing the file and how it was generated. 5. Open a PR against the modules branch. Once merged, update main.nf.test to use params.modules_testdata_base_path + '...' instead of ${projectDir}/tests/data/. 6. Run code-to-module update-standards to add the new path to the standards index so future users of the tool can reference it via Strategy 1.

TODO stubs in main.nf.test — no test data available Channels classified as custom or unknown fall back to Nextflow stub mode. The generated module includes a stub: block and the nf-test runs with -stub. To replace with real data: - Check if a suitable file already exists in nf-core/test-datasets - If not, derive one from existing nf-core data using bcftools/samtools and submit it to nf-core/test-datasets (ask on #test-data Slack first) - Update the TODO comment in main.nf.test with the actual file path

The bundled nf_core_standards.json is more than 30 days old and a newer version was found. Run code-to-module update-standards to fetch it. In CI, suppress the check with --no-update-check to avoid flaky network-dependent behaviour.

update-standards fails: "malformed schema" The downloaded schema failed validation. This usually means the source_url in your current schema points to an incompatible version of the file. File a bug report. Your existing bundled schema is unchanged and the tool continues to work.

Generated module uses wrong label / channel name after nf-core update If nf-core has changed conventions since your last schema update, run code-to-module update-standards to pull the latest schema, then regenerate. If the new conventions aren't in the schema yet, open an issue in the code-to-module repo — this is the expected contribution path.

code-to-module skips the menu when stdin is not a TTY (e.g. piped, CI, subprocess). To force a specific strategy in automation: use --container dockerfile|biocontainers|generate|stub. To see what options are available: code-to-module containers SOURCE.

Expected container option missing from menu Discovery runs all checks — if an option doesn't appear, it wasn't found: - DOCKERFILE missing: no Dockerfile in repo root or one level deep - BIOCONTAINERS missing: tool not found in Bioconda (check https://anaconda.org/bioconda/{tool}) - GENERATED missing: no environment.yml or requirements.txt found in repo

BioContainers default for Tier 3 tool If your Tier 3 custom script happens to share a name with a Bioconda package, BioContainers may appear as default unexpectedly. Use --container dockerfile or --container generate to override, or select a different option interactively.

Dockerfile found but image tag is "latest" code-to-module falls back to "latest" when it can't determine a version from Git tags. Pin a real version: docker build -t ghcr.io/your-org/tool:1.0.0 . Then update the container line in main.nf before submission.

Generated Dockerfile fails to build For GENERATED_FROM_ENVYML: check all conda packages exist in the specified channels. For GENERATED_FROM_REQS: check all pip packages have compatible versions. Run docker build --no-cache to rule out cached layer issues.

Converted Singularity.def container is wrong The .def → Dockerfile conversion is best-effort. Review the generated Dockerfile carefully — %environment and %runscript sections are not automatically converted.

nf-test snapshot mismatches Delete tests/main.nf.test.snap and rerun — nf-test will create a fresh snapshot.

LLM inference returns wrong channel types Override with --tier flag and manually edit main.nf. The tier flag adjusts how much context the LLM is given about nf-core conventions.

nf-core modules lint fails with "template outdated" Run: nf-core modules patch {module_name} to reconcile differences.

nf-core submission rejected: "container must be on BioContainers or quay.io" nf-core requires containers to be publicly accessible and ideally on quay.io/biocontainers. Options: 1. Submit the tool to Bioconda (preferred) — this auto-creates a BioContainers image 2. Build a Mulled container: https://github.com/BioContainers/multi-package-containers 3. Host on quay.io under your own org and request nf-core review Re-run with --container biocontainers once the Bioconda submission is approved.

docs.title: What makes a good test case
docs.text:
nf-core modules wrap command-line invocations — the tool must be independently executable from the shell with file inputs and file outputs. This rules out Python libraries whose public API is importable functions rather than CLI commands. A library like decoupler or liana would first need a CLI wrapper script written for it before code-to-module can do anything useful; generating that wrapper is a future capability (library-to-module), not part of the current scope.

A good test case has: - A real CLI entry point (argparse, click, or a shell script with flags) - File-based I/O (reads a file, writes a file — not stdin/stdout only) - Not already in nf-core/modules - Available on Bioconda or conda-forge (so container discovery has something to find)

docs.title: CLI tools (current scope)
docs.text:
Celltypist — automated cell type annotation. Has a proper CLI (celltypist --help), takes h5ad input, outputs predictions as CSV or h5ad. On conda-forge, not in Bioconda, no nf-core module. Used as the Prompt 16 manual checkpoint. 

https://github.com/Teichlab/celltypist
docs: https://celltypist.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

Scrublet — doublet detection for single-cell RNA-seq. Has a CLI script (scrublet_score_doublets.py), takes a counts matrix (h5 or CSV), outputs doublet scores. Simple enough that discovery should produce a clean Tier 1-2 result — good for verifying the happy path after fixing any issues found with Celltypist. 

https://github.com/swolock/scrublet

docs.title: Library tools (future scope — not yet supported)
docs.text:
These tools have no CLI entry points. Wrapping them as nf-core modules requires first generating a Python wrapper script that parses arguments and calls the library function — a library-to-module capability not yet implemented.

decoupler (scverse) — biological activity inference (transcription factor and pathway scores). Exports submodules (tl, pp, pl etc.) whose public functions (run_ulm, run_mlm, run_aucell) are the real entry points. The repo has moved from saezlab/decoupler-py to scverse/decoupler. 

https://github.com/scverse/decoupler
docs: https://decoupler-py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

liana-py — cell-cell communication inference. Similarly library-only, exports submodule namespaces rather than CLI commands. On Bioconda, which makes it a good future test for the BioContainers path once library-to-module exists. 

https://github.com/saezlab/liana-py
docs: https://liana-py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

Future extension — Option B for --existing-modules: Rather than requiring the user to provide module paths manually, a future --nf-core-tool TOOLNAME flag could auto-fetch all existing nf-core modules for that tool family from the nf-core/modules GitHub API, removing the need to have them checked out locally. This would be particularly useful when adding a new subcommand to a tool that already has several modules in the community repository.

docs.title: Appendix — Lessons Learned and Tool-Specific Knowledge
docs.text:
code-to-module has no built-in mechanism for capturing lessons learned during debugging or review sessions. This is intentional for now — the tool already has two channels for feeding context back into generation:

  • --docs — pass any URL or local file, including a hand-written notes file, to give the LLM context it couldn't infer from code alone.
  • --existing-modules — pass previously generated modules to align container URLs, labels, and channel conventions.

The recommended workaround for tool-specific lessons is a plain markdown file maintained alongside the module:

modules/liana/TOOL_NOTES.md

Annotate it after each session with anything that would have helped generation — version string location, non-standard output paths, known lint failures and their fixes, quirks of the conda package. Pass it back in on future runs:

code-to-module convert liana-py/ --docs modules/liana/TOOL_NOTES.md

Recurring generation mistakes (e.g. the LLM consistently using the wrong container registry, or omitting a required meta.yml field) belong in the standards schema rather than in a notes file. Update standards/data/nf_core_standards.json, bump schema_version, and run code-to-module update-standards. This fixes the mistake for all future modules, not just the next one.

CLI wrapper over library API — a distinct tool category. Some tools present as CLI tools (they have a console_scripts entry point and accept --indata, --model, --outdir style flags) but are primarily Python libraries whose CLI is a thin dispatch wrapper. Celltypist is an example: its README devotes far more space to celltypist.annotate() than to celltypist --indata. These tools have:

  • One console_scripts entry point dispatching via flags (not subcommands)
  • A rich Python API documented in sections separate from the CLI section
  • File-based I/O in the CLI that maps cleanly to nf-core channels
  • Multiple distinct operational modes expressed as flags rather than subcommands

The README's CLI section is the right source for identifying which modes exist. For celltypist: --indata --model --outdir (annotate), --indata ... --majority-voting (annotate + voting), --update-models (download), --show-models (list). These are real distinct modes — the LLM-based splitting into 6 sub-functionalities was semantically reasonable, it just used the wrong mechanism (doc interpretation) instead of code signals.

The correct treatment for pipeline use is two modules: celltypist/annotate and celltypist/train. Utility modes with no file I/O (--update-models, --show-models) are not pipeline steps and should be filtered out during discovery.

Discovery must be code-signal-driven, not LLM-driven. The LLM must never be called during the discovery phase to interpret, expand, or split functionalities. Splitting must come from source code analysis only, following a three-level strategy:

  1. Explicit subcommands (add_subparsers()): one FunctionalitySpec per add_parser("name") call. Name = subcommand name.
  2. Mutually exclusive flag groups or mode-switching flags: parse the AST for add_mutually_exclusive_group() blocks and flags that trigger fundamentally different execution paths. One spec per mode, named toolname_modename. Filter out modes with no file input and no file output — these are not pipeline steps.
  3. Flat parser, no clear mode split: one spec for the whole tool. Name = console_scripts key.

The LLM is only involved from infer.py onward, once a FunctionalitySpec with a real code section has been produced by code-signal-based discovery.

meta must be enforced as a post-processing invariant, not inferred by the LLM. Every nf-core module must accept a meta map as the first input for sample tracking — without it, $meta.id in the tag line and def prefix = task.ext.prefix ?: "${meta.id}" fail at runtime. During the celltypist debugging session, meta was correctly generated in one run and then silently dropped in the next when the optional channel fix changed how infer.py processed the input list. The LLM does not see meta in the tool's source code (it's a Nextflow convention, not a CLI argument) and will therefore omit it whenever it doesn't appear in the prior context.

The fix is a post-processing step in infer.py that runs after the LLM returns a ModuleSpec and enforces the meta convention unconditionally: - If the first input is not val(meta) or tuple val(meta), path(...), insert it - If meta is present but not first, move it to first position - Never allow meta to be optional or dropped

If there is one required path input, combine with meta into a tuple: tuple val(meta), path(input_data). If there are multiple required path inputs, keep meta as a standalone val(meta) on its own line. This is a structural invariant — it should never be left to LLM inference.

Optional CLI arguments must map to optional Nextflow channels. Celltypist's --gene-file and --cell-file are only required when the input is in .mtx format — they are genuinely optional for the common .h5ad and .csv input paths. If infer.py generates these as required channels, the module will fail for any non-mtx input. The fix is path gene_file optional true in main.nf. General rule: any CLI argument marked default=None or guarded by a conditional in the argparse handler should map to an optional channel, not a required one. infer.py should detect this from the AST or from docs context and set optional: true in the channel spec accordingly.

Stub test data strategy is expected for tools requiring domain-specific inputs. Celltypist requires a .pkl model file and an .h5ad or .csv count matrix — neither is in nf-core test-datasets. All input channels therefore fall back to stub strategy in the test data table, which is correct behaviour. The TODO comments in tests/main.nf.test give guidance for adding real test data later. This is not a bug — it reflects the genuine difficulty of testing single-cell tools in a CI environment. The stub strategy produces a syntactically valid test that passes lint and confirms channel wiring without requiring real biological data.

Confidence score ambiguity. The word "confidence" and percentage scores currently appear in two unrelated contexts with different meanings:

  • Discovery (→ Auto-selected: celltypist (confidence 78%)) — how strongly the evidence supports this being a genuine CLI entry point. Should be driven by signal type: console_scripts entry → ~95%, argparse block detected → ~75%, public function name only → ~50%.
  • Assessment (Tier 3 Confidence: 74%) — how clearly the code fits the criteria for the assigned tier. A Tier 3 at 74% means "probably Tier 3 but some signals are ambiguous."

These are different things. A user seeing both assumes they mean the same thing — they don't. Fix before release: rename the assessment score to tier match and display it as a qualitative label (strong / moderate / weak) rather than a percentage, reserving percentages for discovery where they have a clear meaning as a selection threshold.

Tier descriptions missing from output. A user seeing Tier 3 Confidence: 74% with no context has to read the docs to understand what Tier 3 means and whether 74% is good or bad. The tier descriptions are already defined in the standards schema and in assess.py — they just aren't surfaced in the Rich output. Fix before release: add a one-line description to the assessment box:

╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Tier 3  ·  moderate match                           │
│ Has CLI structure but no Bioconda/PyPI container    │
│ ⚠ No container found — stub will be generated      │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

The five tiers for reference:

Tier Meaning 1 CLI tool, in Bioconda, container ready 2 CLI tool, in PyPI/conda-forge, container resolvable 3 CLI tool, container must be built from Dockerfile or generated 4 Ambiguous structure, limited I/O signals 5 Cannot auto-generate — manual creation required

Future extension: If the same tool-specific quirks appear repeatedly across users, a structured tool_knowledge/ directory — written after a successful session, passed in automatically on the next run — would automate what the notes file does manually. Build this only once real-world usage reveals which information is actually worth capturing.

Perl tools and other non-Python wrappers assess as Tier 5. TrimGalore is a Perl wrapper around cutadapt and FastQC — it has no Python AST to analyse, no importable entry points, and its CLI is defined in a Perl script that the rule-based detectors do not parse. It correctly assesses as Tier 5 (cannot auto-generate) and the regression suite skips it rather than producing a misleading low score. This is correct behaviour. General rule: tools whose primary implementation language is not Python, R, or shell will tend toward Tier 4–5 unless they have a very clean argparse- style CLI. The right response is to report the tier honestly and let the user decide whether to proceed manually — not to attempt generation and produce a broken module. For the regression test suite, prefer Python or R tools with Bioconda packages as reference targets; Perl wrappers are poor regression targets regardless of how widely used they are. cutadapt (Python, single-functionality, Bioconda-backed) is a better third regression target than TrimGalore for this reason.

Prompt 29 baseline results (v0.1.0). The first complete run of Prompt 29 against the full codebase produced the following results, which serve as the baseline for future regression comparisons:

Stage Result Notes 1 ✓ PASS 304 passed, 0 failed, 27 deselected (network/llm) 2 ✓ PASS All 8 boundary tests clean 3 ✓ PASS 4/4 chain tests passed 4 ✓ PASS* 10/12 checkpoint items; 2 LLM-output variations (input count 5 vs 6; stub globs too generic) — not code defects 5 ✓ PASS 13/13 robustness tests (celltypist/cutadapt/multiqc/pyfastx) 6 ✓ PASS No drift detected; 4 network tests skipped gracefully 7 ✓ PASS 5/6 LLM invariants held; 1 skipped (empty script_template — correct behaviour) 8 ✓ PASS multiqc ✓, cutadapt ✓, trimgalore SKIP (Perl/Tier 5)

The two Stage 4 variations are expected LLM non-determinism, not regressions. If a future run produces more than 2 checkpoint variations, or if a previously-passing invariant fails in Stage 7, investigate before releasing.

docs.title: Why library tools are out of scope today
docs.text:
nf-core modules wrap command-line invocations. The Nextflow process script shells out to a tool:

script:
"""
celltypist \
    --input $input \
    --model $model \
    --output $prefix.csv
"""

Python libraries whose public API consists of importable functions (decoupler, liana, scanpy) don't have this shape. There is no command to shell out to. Wrapping them requires first generating a CLI wrapper script — a bin/tool.py that parses arguments, calls the library function, and writes output files. That script doesn't exist in the repo; it would need to be created.

docs.title: Why library-to-module, not library-to-tool
docs.text:
The wrapper script is an implementation detail of the module, not a standalone deliverable. nf-core modules can ship a bin/ directory with helper scripts that the process calls — module consumers never see the script directly, they call the process. So the right target is a self-contained module where the bin script is generated inside the module directory as part of the conversion:

library function → bin/tool_name.py (generated) → nf-core module wrapping it

docs.title: Architecture: an extension of code-to-module, not a separate tool
docs.text:
The two paths share significant code — everything from assess onward is identical. Both produce a ModuleSpec and feed it into the same Jinja2 templates. The divergence is only in discovery and a new bin script generation step:

CLI tool:  ingest → discover (CLI entry points) ──────────────────────┐
                                                                       ├→ assess → infer → generate
Library:   ingest → discover (library functions) → bin_gen.py (new) ──┘

The right implementation is a strategy pattern inside discover.py, not a separate tool or a separate ingest path:

# discover.py
def discover(source, mode="auto"):
    if mode == "auto":
        mode = "library" if _looks_like_library(source) else "cli"
    if mode == "cli":
        return _discover_cli(source)      # existing logic
    if mode == "library":
        return _discover_library(source)  # __init__.py expansion

_looks_like_library checks: no argparse/click/shell entry points found AND __init__.py exports functions or submodules rather than a CLI group. The --library-mode flag forces the choice rather than auto-detecting, which is useful when auto-detection gets it wrong.

The only genuinely new file is bin_gen.py, which takes a FunctionalitySpec with a library function signature and generates the bin/ wrapper script. It is called between discover and assess in the library path only. Everything else — ingest.py, assess.py, infer.py, generate.py, container.py, validate.py — is unchanged and shared.

docs.title: Key design decisions when building this
docs.text:
Which functions to wrap. Not every public function in a library deserves an nf-core module. The selection criteria: does the function take file-like inputs and produce file-like outputs when called from a pipeline context? For decoupler, tl.run_ulm(mat, net) takes an AnnData and a network DataFrame — both naturally represented as files (h5ad, csv). Internal helpers, plotting functions, and format converters are lower priority.

The __init__.py submodule expansion logic (detect __all__, expand submodules, extract public functions) developed during the decoupler debugging session is the right starting point for _discover_library. Cap the candidate list early (suggest MAX_LIBRARY_FUNCTIONS = 20) to avoid processing hundreds of internal helpers.

Type-to-format mapping. The hardest inference problem is mapping Python types to file formats. A principled default table:

Python type Nextflow channel type Default format AnnData file .h5ad pd.DataFrame file .csv np.ndarray file .npy str (path) file inferred from name int, float, bool val — dict file .json

This table belongs in standards/data/nf_core_standards.json once built, so it can be updated without code changes.

Version capture. The generated bin script must print the library version to stdout for the nf-core version capture pattern. For pip-installed packages this is always importlib.metadata.version("package_name").

Testing. Because bin_gen.py is the only new module, it needs its own write-first test prompt (following the same pattern as Prompts 5 and 12). The rest of the test suite requires no changes — assess, infer, generate, and validate tests all run against ModuleSpec regardless of how it was produced.

docs.title: Appendix — nf-core Module Regression Tests
docs.text: Note: This appendix is now superseded by Prompt 28, which implements regression.py, RegressionScore, parse_module, and semantic_score as production code with full tests. The background and design rationale below remains useful reading before tackling Prompt 28.

docs.title: The idea
docs.text:
Existing nf-core modules are ground truth. There are hundreds of them with known-correct main.nf, meta.yml, and environment.yml. Running code-to-module convert against the source tool and comparing the output against the reference module gives a reproducible, automatable quality signal that doesn't require human review — and scales across the entire nf-core module catalogue.

This is the eval-driven development loop that makes LLM-based tools reliable over time: generate → compare → identify gap → fix in infer.py or generate.py or add to lessons-learned → repeat. Each failure mode caught becomes a permanent improvement rather than a one-off manual fix.

docs.title: What to compare — semantic diff, not string diff
docs.text:
The LLM will never reproduce exact formatting, variable names, or comment style of a hand-written module. Exact string diffing will always fail. What you can compare is structure and semantics:

Dimension What to check Process name Matches expected (e.g. SAMTOOLS_SORT) Container URL Matches BioContainers reference for the same version Input channels Generated names are a subset of reference inputs; types match Output channels Emit names match reference (these are the public contract) Version capture Pattern present, uses the right command (tool --version) Script block No TODO placeholders; tool invocation references correct input channel names meta.yml keywords Overlap with reference keywords above a threshold

Score each dimension independently. A module that gets container, process name, outputs, and version capture right but has slightly different input channel names is much better than a module that fails on all dimensions — and that distinction is lost in a binary pass/fail.

docs.title: Implementation sketch
docs.text:
# tests/test_regression.py

REFERENCE_MODULES = [
    # (tool_github_url, docs_url, nf_core_module_path)
    ("https://github.com/samtools/samtools", None,
     "nf-core/modules/modules/nf-core/samtools/sort"),
    ("https://github.com/alexdobin/STAR", None,
     "nf-core/modules/modules/nf-core/star/align"),
    # add more as the suite grows
]

@pytest.mark.regression
@pytest.mark.parametrize("tool_url,docs_url,reference_path", REFERENCE_MODULES)
def test_against_reference_module(tool_url, docs_url, reference_path, tmp_path):
    run_convert(tool_url, docs_url, outdir=tmp_path)
    generated = parse_module(tmp_path)
    reference = parse_module(reference_path)
    score = semantic_score(generated, reference)
    assert score.process_name    # must match
    assert score.container_url   # must match
    assert score.output_channels >= 0.8   # 80% of reference outputs present
    assert score.no_todos        # no placeholder comments

parse_module reads main.nf into a structured object (process name, inputs, outputs, container, script body). semantic_score compares two parsed modules dimension by dimension.

docs.title: Selecting reference modules
docs.text:
Not all nf-core modules are equally suitable as regression targets:

  • Prefer recently updated modules — older modules use patterns superseded by current nf-core lint rules. Pin to modules with a last-commit date after the nf-core 2.14 release or that pass nf-core lint cleanly today.
  • Prefer single-functionality tools — samtools/sort, fastqc, trimgalore are cleaner targets than multi-subcommand wrappers like gatk4/haplotypecaller which involve complex conditional logic.
  • Prefer tools with Bioconda packages — these exercise the full BioContainers resolution path, which is the most important container discovery path to keep correct.
  • Avoid modules that wrap scripts or custom containers — these have non-standard patterns that code-to-module isn't designed to replicate.

docs.title: Where this fits in the build order
docs.text:
Regression tests are not part of the initial 22-prompt tutorial — they require a working implementation to generate output to compare against. The right time to build them is after the Prompt 16 manual checkpoint confirms end-to-end generation is working correctly for Celltypist.

Add them as an optional Prompt 23 once the core 22 prompts are stable:

Prompt 23 (optional): Regression test suite
Dependencies: all 22 prompts complete, at least one real-world module
generated and reviewed successfully.
Goal: implement tests/test_regression.py with semantic_score comparator
and 3-5 reference modules from nf-core/modules.
Write-first: define the RegressionScore dataclass and semantic_score
signature before implementing parse_module or the comparator logic.

docs.title: Appendix — Test Data from Tool Documentation (Future Capability: Strategy 2c)
The problem
docs.text:
Strategies 1 and 2 (match and derive) both depend on nf-core/test-datasets as their source of truth. This works well for standard genomics formats — BAM, VCF, FASTA, FASTQ — because nf-core/test-datasets has comprehensive coverage of these. It breaks down for domain-specific formats that the nf-core community hasn't needed yet:

  • Single-cell formats: .h5ad, .loom, .mtx + barcodes/features
  • Spatial transcriptomics: .h5, Visium directories
  • Mass spectrometry: .mzML, .raw
  • Proteomics: .fasta databases, .pin files
  • Machine learning models: .pkl, .pt, .onnx

For Celltypist, the workflow correctly falls through to stub because there is no .h5ad or .pkl in nf-core/test-datasets. But Celltypist's own documentation links to a minimal example dataset used in every tutorial. That dataset is small, publicly available, already used by the tool's authors for testing, and exactly the right semantic content for an nf-test. The workflow currently ignores it entirely.

docs.title: Strategy 2c: fetch from tool documentation
docs.text:
Strategy 2c sits between Strategy 2b (chain) and Strategy 3 (stub) in priority order. It triggers when:

  • Strategy 1 (match) finds no nf-core/test-datasets file for the channel's format
  • Strategy 2b (chain) finds no upstream nf-core module that produces this format
  • The LLM identified a suggested_test_data_url for this channel during inference

If all three conditions hold, the workflow generates a fetch_and_minimise.sh script that downloads the example file and reduces it to a size suitable for CI testing.

docs.title: How it fits into the existing architecture
docs.text:
During infer.py: Add a suggested_test_data_url: str | None field to ChannelSpec. When the LLM is inferring channel specs and --docs content is present, it should look for example dataset links in the documentation and populate this field. The prompt addition to infer.py is small:

For each input channel, if the documentation mentions an example file or dataset
URL that matches this channel's format, set suggested_test_data_url to that URL.
Only set it if the URL points directly to a downloadable file (not a tutorial page).
If no URL is found, leave it as null.

During test_data_derive.py: Add a Strategy 2c branch to plan_derivation():

# Strategy 2c: fetch from tool documentation
if (
    channel.suggested_test_data_url is not None
    and not _is_derivable(channel, standards)   # Strategy 2 already failed
    and not _is_chainable(channel, standards)   # Strategy 2b already failed
):
    return _plan_fetch(channel, standards, tool_name)

_plan_fetch() returns a DerivationPlan with strategy="fetch" and a new fetch_url field. The source enum gains a FETCHED value alongside DERIVED, CHAINED, and STUB.

A new Jinja2 template: templates/derive_test_data/fetch_and_minimise.sh.j2 generates a shell script that:

  1. Downloads the file from suggested_test_data_url using curl -L
  2. Runs a format-appropriate minimisation step (see table below)
  3. Writes the minimised file to tests/data/{tool_name}_test.{ext}
  4. Prints PR instructions for nf-core/test-datasets

Minimisation by format:

Format Minimisation tool Target size Command sketch .h5ad anndata (Python) ~100 cells, ~500 genes adata[:100, :500].write_h5ad(out) .loom loompy (Python) ~100 cells loompy.combine([ds[:, :100]], out) .mtx scipy.sparse first 100 rows slice + mmwrite .pkl none — keep as-is — copy only; models can't be meaningfully subset .mzML pyteomics first N spectra parse + rewrite

For .pkl and other opaque binary formats where minimisation isn't possible, the script downloads the full file with a size warning and a note that it may be too large for nf-core/test-datasets.

docs.title: License checking
docs.text:
Before generating the fetch script, the workflow should inspect the docs page for license terms. The LLM can do this as part of the suggested_test_data_url extraction step:

If you identify a suggested_test_data_url, also set suggested_test_data_license
to the license of the example dataset if you can determine it from the docs page.
Use "unknown" if you cannot determine it. Use "cc0", "mit", "apache2", "gpl",
or "proprietary" as values where known.

The fetch script is generated regardless, but includes a prominent comment:

# LICENSE: {{ license }}
# IMPORTANT: Verify this dataset's license before submitting to nf-core/test-datasets.
# nf-core/test-datasets requires permissive licensing (CC0, CC-BY, or equivalent).
# Ask on Slack #test-data if unsure.

If license == "proprietary", the script is not generated and the channel falls through to stub with a specific message: "Example data identified but license is incompatible with nf-core/test-datasets. Using stub mode."

docs.title: What this would have done for Celltypist
docs.text:
With Strategy 2c in place, the Celltypist run would have:

  1. During inference, identified https://celltypist.cog.suse.com/models/models.zip (or the readthedocs tutorial dataset link) as suggested_test_data_url for the model channel, and a PBMC example .csv or .h5ad for the input channel
  2. Generated fetch_and_minimise.sh that downloads the PBMC example and subsets it to 100 cells using anndata
  3. Produced a working nf-test referencing tests/data/celltypist_test.h5ad instead of a stub block
  4. Flagged the model .pkl as non-minimisable with a size warning

The nf-test would still need the user to run fetch_and_minimise.sh and PR the file to nf-core/test-datasets — but it would be a real, runnable test rather than a stub placeholder.

docs.title: Where this fits in the build order
docs.text:
Strategy 2c requires: - Prompt 8 (infer.py) — adds suggested_test_data_url to ChannelSpec - Prompt 11 (test_data_derive.py) — adds the _plan_fetch() branch

It should be implemented as an extension to Prompt 11 once the core three strategies are stable. It is more complex than Strategies 1–3 because it involves: - A new field in the LLM inference prompt - A new DerivationPlan strategy value - A new Jinja2 template with format-specific minimisation logic - A license check heuristic

For that reason it is scoped here as a future capability rather than part of the core 28 prompts. The right time to build it is after Prompt 16 (the Celltypist checkpoint) confirms that stub mode is the actual bottleneck for real-world tools.

docs.title: Proposed prompt
docs.text:
Prompt 11b (optional): Strategy 2c — fetch test data from tool documentation

Dependencies: Prompts 8 and 11 complete and green.

Goal: When a channel has no nf-core/test-datasets match and no chain module,
but the LLM identified a documentation URL for example data, generate a
fetch_and_minimise.sh script that downloads and minimises the file.

Write-first:
  - Add suggested_test_data_url: str | None to ChannelSpec in models.py
  - Add test_fetch_plan_for_h5ad_url: ChannelSpec with format_tags=["h5ad"]
    and suggested_test_data_url set → plan has strategy="fetch", fetch_url set
  - Add test_fetch_falls_through_to_stub_when_proprietary: license="proprietary"
    → strategy="stub", message mentions license incompatibility
  - Add test_fetch_script_contains_anndata_minimisation: rendered script
    contains "adata[:" for h5ad format

Then implement:
  1. Add suggested_test_data_url and suggested_test_data_license to ChannelSpec
  2. Add "fetch" to the strategy Literal and FETCHED to TestDataSource
  3. Add fetch_url field to DerivationPlan
  4. Add _plan_fetch() to test_data_derive.py
  5. Create templates/derive_test_data/fetch_and_minimise.sh.j2
  6. Update test_gen.py orchestrator to handle FETCHED plans
  7. Update the LLM prompt in infer.py to populate the two new fields

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
Tools that assess as Tier 4–5

Perl wrappers, tools with no detectable CLI structure, and tools that require large proprietary databases (BLAST, Kraken2) typically assess as Tier 4 or 5. The tool reports the tier honestly and generates what it can; Tier 5 tools require manual module authoring.

TrimGalore is a representative example: it is a Perl wrapper around cutadapt and FastQC with no Python AST to analyse. It correctly assesses as Tier 5 and the tool stops with an explanation rather than producing a misleading partial module. For these tools, use the assessment output to understand what the tool found, then author the module manually using nf-core modules create as a starting point.

Library-only tools (no CLI entry point)

Tools like decoupler and liana-py expose a Python API but no command-line interface. These are not yet supported. code-to-module works by analysing CLI structure — console_scripts entry points, argparse/Click argument parsers, shell case statements — and cannot generate a meaningful module for code that is designed to be called as import foo; foo.run(...) rather than foo --input file --output dir.

Library-to-module support is planned — see the development tutorial for the proposed architecture. The intended approach is to generate a thin CLI wrapper script first, then feed that into the standard conversion pipeline.

Domain-specific test data

Formats not in nf-core/test-datasets (h5ad, pkl, mzML, Visium directories) fall back to stub mode. Real test data must be added manually and PRed to nf-core/test-datasets before submission.

Celltypist is a representative example: its inputs (.pkl model files and .h5ad or .csv count matrices) are not in nf-core/test-datasets. All input channels in tests/main.nf.test receive stub strategy, which produces a syntactically valid test that passes lint and confirms channel wiring — but it cannot test real data flow. The TODO comments in the generated test file describe exactly what data would be needed.

LLM non-determinism

Running convert twice on the same tool may produce slightly different channel names, output globs, or script arguments. This is expected behaviour — always review the generated module before submitting. The post-processing guards in infer.py enforce structural invariants (meta as first input, no duplicate versions emit, no TODO placeholders) but do not guarantee identical output across runs.

If two runs produce significantly different modules, the tool-specific context is ambiguous and both outputs deserve manual review. Passing --docs with the tool's documentation URL typically reduces variation by giving the LLM more signal to work with.

docs.title: Planned improvements
docs.text:
The following are known future directions. Contributions are welcome — see GitHub Issues for open items.

  • Library-to-module: generate a CLI wrapper for library-only Python tools, then pipe the result through the standard conversion pipeline.
  • Strategy 2c (test data from tool docs): if tool documentation links to example data files of appropriate size, use them directly rather than deriving from nf-core/test-datasets.
  • Snakemake-to-Nextflow: convert Snakemake rules to nf-core module format, using the rule's input/output blocks to infer channel structure instead of CLI analysis.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install from PyPI:

pip install code-to-module

Set your Anthropic API key before running convert. The other commands do not call the API:

export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

Devcontainer: a .devcontainer/devcontainer.json is included with all Python dependencies, nf-core/tools, and nf-test pre-installed. Open the repo in VS Code or GitHub Codespaces to get a fully-configured environment without a local install.

External tools for validation: validate-module test and validate-module fix require nf-test and Docker or Singularity. validate-module review and all code-to-module commands work without them.

docs.title: Your first module
docs.text: This example uses Celltypist, a cell type annotation tool with a genuine CLI, a Bioconda package, and no existing nf-core module — a good real-world target.

docs.title: Step 1 — Convert
docs.text:
code-to-module convert https://github.com/Teichlab/celltypist \
  --outdir modules/ \
  --docs https://celltypist.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ \
  --no-interaction

Pass --docs to give the LLM context it could not infer from source code alone — documentation URLs or local markdown files are both accepted. --no-interaction skips the container selection menu and uses the tier-aware default.

Confirmed terminal output:

→ Single functionality: celltypist
Tier 2  Confidence: 89%
Selected: BioContainers → quay.io/biocontainers/celltypist:1.7.1--pyhdfd78af_0
→ CELLTYPIST: 6 inputs, 5 outputs
✓ Quick lint passed

Reading the output:

  • Single functionality — the discovery phase found one CLI entry point (celltypist). If multiple subcommands or scripts were found, a selection UI would appear.
  • Tier 2 — the tool is in Bioconda with minor inference ambiguity. BioContainers is selected as the default container.
  • 6 inputs, 5 outputs — the number of inferred channel specs. These may need review; optional inputs in particular should be marked optional true in main.nf.
  • Quick lint passed — no structural problems detected before running nf-core lint.

docs.title: Step 2 — Review the generated files
docs.text:
modules/
└── celltypist/
    ├── main.nf                 # Process definition
    ├── meta.yml                # Module metadata, channel descriptions, EDAM terms
    ├── environment.yml         # Conda environment pinned to container version
    └── tests/
        ├── main.nf.test        # nf-test spec
        └── nextflow.config     # ext.args wiring

Open main.nf and check:

  • Output globs match what the tool actually produces (e.g. *.pkl, *.csv)
  • Optional CLI arguments are marked optional true
  • The process label (process_medium) matches the tool's typical resource use
  • ext.args is wired correctly in tests/nextflow.config

docs.title: Step 3 — Static review
docs.text:
validate-module review modules/celltypist/

This runs static analysis only — no test execution. It reports three severity levels:

  • ERROR — blocks nf-core submission; must fix before opening a PR
  • WARNING — should fix; reviewers will ask about it
  • INFO — suggestion; optional

Fix any ERROR items manually, re-run the review to confirm, then proceed.

docs.title: Step 4 — Test and fix
docs.text:
validate-module test modules/celltypist/
validate-module fix modules/celltypist/

See The validate-module suite below for details.

docs.title: Step 5 — Test data and submission
docs.text:
If derive_test_data.sh was written alongside the module, it means Strategy 2a applied: the test data can be derived from an existing nf-core/test-datasets file but needs a separate PR. Run the script, inspect its output, and PR the derived file to nf-core/test-datasets before submitting the module.

For Celltypist specifically, all inputs fall back to stub strategy because .pkl model files and .h5ad count matrices are not in nf-core/test-datasets. The TODO comments in tests/main.nf.test describe what real test data would look like. Add it to nf-core/test-datasets first, then update the test paths.

When ready, open a module PR following the nf-core modules contributing guide.

docs.title: Test data strategies
docs.text:
The tool tries four strategies in priority order. It stops at the first one that produces a usable test input for each channel.

Strategy Name When it applies 1 Match A file matching the required format and size is already in nf-core/test-datasets. Always preferred. 2a Derive No exact match, but a suitable file can be subsetted or transformed from an existing nf-core/test-datasets file (VCF region, FASTA extract, BAM subsample). Generates derive_test_data.sh. 2b Chain The input is the natural output of a known upstream nf-core module. Generates a setup {} block in main.nf.test. Use only when the input would be too large to store, or when the upstream module runs in seconds (index generation). 3 Stub Data is too large, proprietary, or cannot be derived. Generates a stub: block; tests run with nf-test --stub. Always produces a syntactically valid test.

Strategy 2b (chain) is intentionally lower priority than 2a (derive). Stored files keep CI test runtime predictable; setup {} blocks add upstream module execution time to every test run. Use chain only when a stored file is genuinely not appropriate.

For derive and chain cases, the test data file or the upstream module must be PRed to nf-core/test-datasets before the module PR can be merged.

docs.title: test
docs.text:
validate-module test runs nf-core modules lint and nf-test against the module, captures the output, and classifies each failure into Class A, B, or C. Results are written to stdout as a formatted TestReport; pass --json-output to save them for use with fix.

validate-module test modules/celltypist/
validate-module test modules/celltypist/ --lint-only
validate-module test modules/celltypist/ --json-output report.json

docs.title: fix
docs.text:
validate-module fix loads a test report (or re-runs validation), shows a coloured diff panel for each fixable failure, and waits for explicit approval before writing any file. Every diff is labelled with the fix source:

Class Label What triggers it Auto-fixable A [rule] Deterministic structural issue: wrong emit name, missing topic: versions tag, container URL format, ext.args pattern, meta.yml field Yes, with approval B [llm] Requires reading code in context: wrong output glob, wrong process label, unclear channel description Yes, with approval — lower trust C [manual] Cannot be resolved safely without human judgement Never — explains what to do and stops

The fix command never silently modifies files. At each diff you choose: y apply, n skip, a apply all remaining Class A, s skip all remaining, q quit. Validation re-runs automatically after fixes are applied unless --no-revalidate is passed.

validate-module fix modules/celltypist/
validate-module fix modules/celltypist/ --from-report report.json
validate-module fix modules/celltypist/ --class-a-only
validate-module fix modules/celltypist/ -y    # auto-approve Class A; Class B still prompts

docs.title: review
docs.text:
validate-module review performs static analysis only — no subprocess, no test execution. It checks channel naming conventions, process label appropriateness, ext.args usage, meta.yml completeness, versions channel structure, and EDAM ontology coverage.

validate-module review modules/celltypist/
validate-module review modules/celltypist/ --errors-only
validate-module review modules/celltypist/ --json-output review.json

Example output:

CELLTYPIST — review
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ERROR    versions channel missing 'topic: versions' tag
  WARNING  process label 'process_medium' may be low for h5ad inputs
  INFO     EDAM term not found for .pkl input — add manually if known
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  1 error (blocks submission) · 1 warning · 1 info
